elements.sent-2411-001: Colorectal cancer (CRC) poses a major public health challenge due to its increasing prevalence, particularly among younger populations, with it being the third most commonly diagnosed cancer and the second leading cause of cancer-related deaths worldwide as of 2024.
elements.sent-2411-002: Microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H) CRC and deficient mismatch repair (dMMR) CRC comprise 15\% of all CRC, and have shown remarkable response to immunotherapy, especially with PD-1 inhibitors.
elements.sent-2411-003: Despite this, there is still a significant need to optimise immunotherapeutic regimens to maximise clinical efficacy and patient quality of life, whilst minimising monetary costs.
elements.sent-2411-004: Mathematical models are a promising avenue for treatment optimisation, and we use delay integro-differential equations to model the interactions and dynamics of various cell types, immune checkpoints, damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs), and cytokines.
elements.sent-2411-005: We provide deterministic equations for the concentrations of cancer cells, DAMPs, CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, dendritic cells (DCs), cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs), neutrophils, macrophages, NK cells and various cytokines of pro-inflammatory (IL-2, IFN-$\upgamma$, TNF, IL-12, IL-1$\upbeta$) and immunosuppressive (TGF-$\upbeta$, IL-10, IL-4, IL-6) nature.
elements.sent-2411-006: We consider these cells and their phenotypes, noting that many of these cell types are being modelled deterministically for the first time in cancer, paving the way for a deeper understanding of the complex underlying immune dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-007: We consider two compartments: the tumour site and the tumour-draining lymph node (TDLN), taking into account phenomena such as DC migration, T cell proliferation, acquired resistance to pembrolizumab, and CD8+ T cell exhaustion and reinvigoration.
elements.sent-2411-008: The parameter values and initial conditions for the model are estimated from experimental data, incorporating various pharmacokinetic, bioanalytical, and radiographic studies, as well as deconvolution of bulk RNA-sequencing data from the TCGA COADREAD database.
elements.sent-2411-009: We finally optimise CRC treatment with neoadjuvant pembrolizumab --- one of the most commonly used PD-1 inhibitors.
elements.sent-2411-010: We maximise treatment efficacy and efficiency in locally advanced MSI-H/dMMR CRC patients whilst accounting for toxicity, and improve upon currently FDA-approved therapeutic regimens for metastatic MSI-H/dMMR CRC.
elements.sent-2411-011: We demonstrate that a single medium-to-high dose of pembrolizumab is sufficient for durable tumour eradication, whilst being efficient, safe and practical.
elements.sent-2411-012: Colorectal cancer (CRC) is the third most common cancer worldwide accounting for approximately $10\%$ of all cancer cases \cite{Klimeck2023 with more than 1.85 million cases and 850,000 deaths annually \cite{Biller2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The American Cancer Society estimates that in the United States, there will be 152,810 new cases of CRC diagnosed and 53,010 deaths due to CRC \cite{Siegel2024 with individual risk factors including a family history of CRC, inflammatory bowel disease, and type 2 diabetes \cite{Gausman2020 .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Despite CRC being diagnosed mostly in adults 65 and older, there has been an increase in the incidence rate of CRC amongst younger populations \cite{SifakiPistolla2022, Siegel2023, Siegel2024 since the mid-1990s, with CRC being the leading cause of cancer-related deaths in adults under 55 \cite{Siegel2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: In particular, since many people will not experience symptoms in the early stages of CRC, diagnoses often occur at a later stage when the disease is more advanced, where treatment is significantly less effective, and survival is much worse \cite{Andrew2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Of new CRC diagnoses, 20\% of patients present with metastatic disease, while an additional 25\% who initially have localised disease eventually develop metastases \cite{Biller2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: In the United States, the 5-year survival rates for stage IIIA, stage IIIB, and Stage IIIC colon cancer are 90\%, 72\%, and 53\% respectively, whilst stage IV CRC has a 5-year survival of only 12\% \cite{Rawla2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Noting that folinic acid (leucovorin), a vitamin B derivative, increases the cytotoxicity of 5-fluorouracil \cite{moran1989leucovorin , and with the approval of the topoisomerase I inhibitor irinotecan in 1996 and the platinum-based agent oxaliplatin, mainstay chemotherapy regimens such as FOLFOX (folinic acid, 5-fluorouracil, oxaliplatin) and FOLFIRI (folinic acid, 5-fluorouracil, irinotecan) have become integral to the treatment of advanced CRC \cite{MohelnikovaDuchonova2014 .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: However, the response rate of advanced CRC patients with 5-fluorouracil monotherapy remains at only 10 -- 15\%, with the addition of other anti-cancer drugs increasing response rates to only 40–50\% \cite{Gu2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: MSI-H CRC is associated with the inactivation of mismatch repair (MMR) genes, including MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, and PMS2, leading to deficient MMR (dMMR) and impaired recognition and correction of spontaneous mutations by cells \cite{MuletMargalef2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: In particular, we note that in CRC, MSI-H and dMMR tumours are equivalent \cite{Boland1998-nd , and we denote these tumours as MSI-H/dMMR for the remainder of this work.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Approximately 20\% of stage II, 12\% of stage III, and 4\% of stage IV CRC tumours are diagnosed as MSI-H/dMMR \cite{Sinicrope2011, Venderbosch2014 , with approximately 80\% of sporadic MSI-H/dMMR CRC caused by MLH1 promoter hypermethylation \cite{Andr2020 .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: This leads to a highly increased mutational rate, with MSI-H/dMMR CRC tumours having 10 -- 100 times more somatic tumours compared to microsatellite stable (MSS) CRC tumours \cite{MuletMargalef2023 , resulting in increased tumour mutation burden (TMB) and neoantigen load, and immunogenic tumour microenvironment (TME) with dense immune cell infiltration \cite{Llosa2015, Giannakis2016 .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: This immunogenicity results in patients with MSI-H/dMMR CRC having good prognosis for immunotherapy treatment, in particular to immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) \cite{Ciardiello2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: CTLA-4 is expressed on activated T and B cells and plays a major role in downmodulating the initial stages of T cell activation and proliferation \cite{Buchbinder2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: PD-1, a cell membrane receptor that is expressed on a variety of cell types including activated T cells, activated B cells and monocytes, has been extensively researched in the context of cancer such as MSI-H/dMMR CRC \cite{Sarshekeh2018, Yaghoubi2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: When PD-1 interacts with its ligands (PD-L1 and PD-L2), effector T cell activity is inhibited, resulting in downregulation of pro-inflammatory cytokine secretion and upregulation of immunosuppressive regulatory T cells (Tregs) \cite{Lin2024, han2020pd .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Cancers can exploit this by expressing PD-L1 themselves, evading immunosurveillance, and impairing the proliferation and activity of cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CTLs) \cite{Oliveira2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Blockade of PD-1/PD-L1 complex formation reinvigorates effector T cell activity, resulting in enhanced anti-tumour immunity and responses, leading to improved clinical outcomes in cancer patients \cite{Lee2015, Zhang2020nature .}
elements.sent-2411-030: In the trial, 307 treatment-naive metastatic MSI-H/dMMR CRC patients were randomly assigned to receive pembrolizumab at a dose of 200 mg every 3 weeks or 5–fluorouracil–based chemotherapy every 2 weeks.
elements.sent-2411-031: A partial or complete response was observed in 43.8\% of patients allocated to pembrolizumab therapy, compared with 33.1\% of patients participating in 5-fluorouracil-based therapy.
elements.sent-2411-032: Furthermore, among patients who responded, 83\% in the pembrolizumab group maintained response at 24 months, compared with 35\% of patients receiving chemotherapy.
elements.sent-2411-033: These results motivated the FDA to approve pembrolizumab for the first-line treatment of unresectable or metastatic MSI-H/dMMR CRC on June 29, 2020 \cite{Casak2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: One such phase II study is the NEOPRISM-CRC, NCT05197322, 31 patients with a high TMR and high-risk stage II or stage III MSI-H/dMMR CRC were given 3 cycles of pembrolizumab, at a dose of 200mg every 3 weeks via IV infusion, and underwent surgery 4 -- 6 weeks after the last dose was administered \cite{Shiu2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Seventeen patients exhibited pathologic complete responses (pCRs) (55\%, 95\% CI 36\% -- 73\%), with the remaining patients having their tumours removed after surgery.
elements.sent-2411-036: After a median follow-up time of 6 months, recurrence was found in no patients, and the median cancer-free period was 9.7 months.
elements.sent-2411-037: As part of this, 27 MSI-H/dMMR CRC patients with locally advanced cancer were either given 200 mg pembrolizumab via IV infusion every 3 weeks for eight treatments followed by surgical resection, or 200 mg pembrolizumab via IV infusion every 3 weeks for 16 treatments.
elements.sent-2411-038: Of the 14 MSI-H/dMMR CRC patients who chose the resection approach, 11 of these had pCRs, and overall, 21 MSI-H/dMMR patients exhibited pcR.
elements.sent-2411-039: Additionally, after a median follow-up time of 9.5 months, only 2 patients who underwent surgical resection experienced recurrence or progression.
elements.sent-2411-040: A retrospective study by Dubé-Pelletier et al. of 80 patients with advanced non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) who received 4 mg/kg pembrolizumab every 6 weeks and 80 NSCLC patients who received 2 mg/kg pembrolizumab every 3 weeks, revealed that both therapies were comparable in terms of OS, toxicity and progression-free survival \cite{DubPelletier2023 , despite the less frequent therapy being more cost-effective.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Various pharmacokinetic models have been developed to optimise ICI therapy \cite{Lindauer2016, Shang2022, Yan2023, Wang2020 , with \cite{Puszkiel2024 showing that tripling the dosing interval of nivolumab, another anti-PD-1 antibody, from 240mg every 2 weeks to 240mg every 6 weeks leads to comparable efficacy despite financial costs decreasing threefold.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Mathematical models provide a powerful framework for optimising treatment regimens, and in this work, we construct a comprehensive data-driven model of the immunobiology of MSI-H/dMMR CRC using delay integro-differential equations and use this to evaluate and optimise neoadjuvant pembrolizumab therapy in locally advanced MSI-H/dMMR CRC.
elements.sent-2411-043: Kirshtein et al. developed an ordinary differential equation (ODE) model of CRC progression incorporating immunological components such as T helper cells, Tregs, dendritic cells (DCs), macrophages, as well as considered the effects of carcinogenic cytokines and immunosuppressive agents \cite{Kirshtein2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-044: They used data from the TCGA COADREAD database \cite{COADREAD2012 to perform estimates for the steady states and initial conditions of model variables, and considered data from all patients, regardless of TNM stage.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Moreover, this model was extended in \cite{Budithi2021 to include FOLFIRI treatment.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Bozkurt et al. presented a relatively simple ODE model of CRC treatment with anthracycline doxorubicin, and IL-2 immunotherapy, modelling cancer cells, natural killer (NK) cells, CD8+ T cells, and other lymphocytes \cite{Bozkurt2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-047: We now summarise a few pre-existing differential-equation-based models of PD-1 blockade therapies.
elements.sent-2411-048: Lai et al. modelled the effects of anti-PD-1 and vaccines on cancer, taking into account DC maturation by high mobility group box 1 (HMGB1) and interleukin-2 (IL-2) and interleukin-12 (IL-12) in \cite{Lai2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-049: This model was adapted in \cite{Lai2019 to optimise combination PD-1 and vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) inhibitor therapies in cancer.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Siewe et al. modelled how transforming growth factor beta (TGF-$\upbeta$) can be used to overcome resistance to PD-1 blockade and also incorporated macrophages, Tregs, IL-2, IL-12, TGF-$\upbeta$, interleukin-10 (IL-10), and chemokine ligand 2 (CCL-2) \cite{Siewe2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-051: This model was extended in \cite{Siewe2023 to model cancer therapy with PD-1 inhibitors with CSF-1 blockade, also including the cytokine tumour necrosis factor (TNF).}
elements.sent-2411-052: Additionally, Liao et al. constructed a mathematical model that demonstrated the pro-cancer or anti-cancer nature of interleukin-27 (IL-27) in combination with anti-PD-1, incorporating the following cytokines: IL-27, TGF-$\upbeta$, IL-2, interferon-gamma (IFN-$\upgamma$), and IL-10 \cite{Liao2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-053: One of the biggest issues is that mature DC migration to the tumour-draining lymph node (TDLN) to activate naive T cells is not addressed, with T cell proliferation and migration to the tumour site (TS) also not being addressed.
elements.sent-2411-054: Kumbhari et al. attempted to address this in \cite{Kumbhari2020 and \cite{Kumbhari2020n2 in the context of optimising cancer vaccine therapy; however, activation is treated as occurring instantaneously which has been shown to be false experimentally \cite{Plambeck2022 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: Moreover, since T cell activation and proliferation take a non-negligible amount of time to occur, immune checkpoint inhibition of these processes must take this into account.
elements.sent-2411-056: No papers to date have properly considered this inhibition deterministically throughout the whole proliferation and activation programs, since examining inhibition at a single moment in time is insufficient to characterise this properly.
elements.sent-2411-057: Additionally, damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs), released by necrotic cancer cells, induce DC maturation.
elements.sent-2411-058: To date, DC maturation is considered only by HMGB1 in some models, with other important DAMPs such as calreticulin and extracellular ATP not being taken into account \cite{DelPrete2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-059: Furthermore, no deterministic model to date has adequately addressed CD8+ T cell exhaustion due to prolonged antigen exposure \cite{Blank2019 , nor their potential reinvigoration through immune checkpoint blockade.}
elements.sent-2411-060: These include neutrophils, which can either enhance anti-tumour immunity or reduce pro-tumour inflammation \cite{Hedrick2021, UstyanovskaAvtenyuk2020 , cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) which are the most abundant component of the TME and produce immunosuppressive cytokines \cite{Zhang2023, Mao2021 , and cytokines such as interleukin-4 (IL-4), interleukin-6 (IL-6) and interleukin-1 beta (IL-1$\upbeta$) \cite{Briukhovetska2021 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: One thing to note is that pre-existing deterministic models mostly estimate cytokine production parameters via biologically informed assumptions, which can lead to inaccuracies and is a somewhat ad hoc approach.
elements.sent-2411-062: In this work we construct a mathematical model using data-driven delay integro-differential equations that addresses these drawbacks, incorporating all of the aforementioned processes and species, and use this to optimise neoadjuvant pembrolizumab therapy in locally advanced MSI-H/dMMR CRC.
elements.sent-2411-063: T cell activation occurs in the lymph node and occurs through T cell receptor (TCR) recognition of cancer antigen presented by major histocompatibility complex (MHC) class I molecules, in the case of CD8+ T cells, and MHC class II molecules, in the case of CD4+ T cells, expressed on the surfaces of mature DCs \cite{Shah2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-064: Cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CTLs) recognise cancer cells through TCR detection of peptide major histocompatibility complexes (pMHCs) on cancer cell surfaces via MHC class I \cite{Sugiyarto2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-065: CD8+ cells, as well as NK cells, are amongst the most cytotoxic and important cells in cancer cell lysis \cite{Maimela2019 , in addition to secreting pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-2, IFN-$\upgamma$, and TNF \cite{Hoekstra2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-066: These are also secreted by CD4+ T helper 1 (Th1) cells, and are an important part of cell-mediated immunity, allowing for neutrophil chemotaxis and macrophage activation \cite{Caza2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-067: On the other hand, CD4+ T helper 2 (Th2) cells produce various anti-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-4, IL-10, and interleukin 23 (IL-23), which induce increased antibody production, and eosinophil activation \cite{Nakayama2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-068: Furthermore, we must also consider CD4+ Tregs, which are vital in immune tissue homeostasis, since they are able to suppress the synthesis of pro-inflammatory cytokines, and control intestinal inflammatory processes \cite{Furiati2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-069: This is done in a variety of ways, including the production of immunomodulatory and immunosuppressive cytokines such as TGF-$\upbeta$, IL-10, and interleukin 35 (IL-35) \cite{Jarnicki2006, Turnis2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-070: We note that naive CD4+ T cells can differentiate towards multiple additional phenotypes such as Th9, Th22, Tfh and Th17 cells, each involved in the pathogenesis of cancer \cite{Cui2019n2, Hetta2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-071: Naive macrophages, denoted M0 macrophages, can differentiate into two main phenotypes: classically activated M1 macrophages and alternatively activated M2 macrophages.
elements.sent-2411-072: These names were given since M1 macrophages promote Th1 cell responses, and M2 macrophages promote Th2 responses, with Th1-associated cytokines downregulating M2 activity, and vice-versa \cite{Mills2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-073: M1 macrophages contribute to the inflammatory response by activating endothelial cells, promoting the induction of nitric oxide synthase, and producing large amounts of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as TNF, IL-1$\upbeta$, and IL‑12 \cite{Viola2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-074: On the other hand, M2 macrophages are responsible for wound healing and the resolution of inflammation, through phagocytosing apoptotic cells, and releasing anti-inflammatory mediators such as IL-10, interleukin 13$\upalpha$1 (IL-13$\upalpha$1), and CC Motif Chemokine Ligand 17 (CCL17) \cite{Han2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-075: Similarly to macrophages, naive neutrophils, denoted N0 neutrophils, can differentiate into two main phenotypes: anti-tumour N1 neutrophils and pro-tumour N2 neutrophils \cite{Fridlender2009 .}
elements.sent-2411-076: N1 neutrophils in CRC are characterised by their production of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as TNF, IL-12, and IL-1$\upbeta$ \cite{Mihaila2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-077: However, N2 neutrophils promote tumour growth and metastasis through the secretion of pro-angiogenic factors and immunosuppressive chemokines such as arginase, CCL2 and chemokine ligand 4 (CCL4) \cite{Masucci2019, Fridlender2009 .}
elements.sent-2411-078: Macrophages and neutrophils are highly plastic and have been demonstrated to integrate environmental signals to change their phenotype and physiology \cite{Xue2014, Giese2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-079: To account for this, in the model, we incorporate macrophage and neutrophil polarisation and repolarisation between their anti-tumour and immunosuppressive phenotypes by various cytokines and proteins.
elements.sent-2411-080: The variables and their units in the model are shown in \autoref{modelvars .}
elements.sent-2411-081: For simplicity, we ignore spatial effects in the model, ignoring the effects of diffusion, advection, and chemotaxis by all species.
elements.sent-2411-082: We assume the system has two compartments: one at the TS, located in the colon or rectum, and one at the tumour-draining lymph node (TDLN).
elements.sent-2411-083: We assume that cytokines in the TS are produced only by effector or activated cells and that DAMPs in the TS are only produced by necrotic cancer cells.
elements.sent-2411-084: We assume that all mature DCs in the TDLN are cancer-antigen bearing and that all T cells in the TS are primed with cancer antigens.
elements.sent-2411-085: Furthermore, we assume that all activated T cells in the TDLN are activated with cancer antigens and that T cell proliferation/division follows a deterministic program.
elements.sent-2411-086: We assume that CD4+ T cells, immediately upon activation, differentiate into the Th1, Th2, or Treg phenotypes, ignoring CD4+ and CD8+ memory T cells.
elements.sent-2411-087: We also assume that all Tregs in the TS are natural Tregs (nTregs), ignoring induced Tregs (iTregs).
elements.sent-2411-088: We assume, for simplicity, that activated neutrophils polarise into the N1/N2 dichotomy, and that activated macrophages polarise into the M1/M2 dichotomy.
elements.sent-2411-089: We also assume that the duration of pembrolizumab infusion is negligible compared to the timescale of the model.
elements.sent-2411-090: Therefore, we treat their infusions as an intravenous bolus so that drug absorption occurs immediately after infusion.
elements.sent-2411-091: Finally, to ensure that all concentrations stay positive regardless of the initial conditions, for biological realism, we assume that cells do not interact before $t=0$, setting their concentrations to nil for $t<0$.
elements.sent-2411-092: Quantities in the top box are in units of $\mathrm{cell/{cm ^3}$, quantities in the second and bottom box are in units of $\mathrm{g/{cm ^3}$, and all other quantities are in units of $\mathrm{molec/{cm ^3}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: All quantities refer to tumour site density/concentration unless otherwise specified.
elements.sent-2411-094: TDLN denotes the tumour-draining lymph node, whilst TS denotes the tumour site.
elements.sent-2411-095: We assume that the rate of activation/polarisation of a species $X_i$ by a species $X_j$ follows the Michaelis-Menten kinetic law $\lambda_{X_i X_j X_i\frac{X_j {K_{X_i X_j +X_j}$, for rate constant $\lambda_{X_i X_j}$, and half-saturation constant $K_{X_i X_j}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: Similarly, we model the rate of inhibition of a species $X_i$ by a species $X_j$ using a term with form $\lambda_{X_i X_j}\frac{X_i {1+X_j/K_{X_i X_j }$ for rate constant $\lambda_{X_i X_j}$, and half-saturation constant $K_{X_i X_j}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-097: Production of $X_i$ by $X_j$ is modelled using mass-action kinetics unless otherwise specified so that the rate that $X_i$ is formed is given by $\lambda_{X_iX_j X_iX_j$ for some positive constant $\lambda_{X_iX_j}$.}
elements.sent-2411-098: Finally, we assume that the rate of lysis of $X_i$ by $X_j$ follows mass-action kinetics in the case where $X_j$ is a cell, and follows Michaelis-Menten kinetics in the case where $X_j$ is a cytokine.
elements.sent-2411-099: We now outline some of the main processes accounted for in the model, with all processes and equations being explained in \Cref{modeleqnssection .}
elements.sent-2411-100: CD8+ T cells, CD4+ T cells, and NK cells induce apoptosis of cancer cells with this being inhibited by proteins including TGF-$\upbeta$, IL-6, PD-1 and PD-L1.
elements.sent-2411-101: However, TNF and IFN-$\upgamma$ induce necroptosis of cancer cells, causing them to become necrotic before they are removed.
elements.sent-2411-102: Necrotic cancer cells release DAMPs such as HMGB1, calreticulin, and extracellular ATP which stimulate immature DCs to mature.
elements.sent-2411-103: Some mature DCs migrate to the T cell zone of the TDLN and activate naive CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, with this being inhibited by PD-1 and CTLA-4.
elements.sent-2411-104: Activated CD4+ and CD8+ T cells undergo clonal expansion and proliferate rapidly in the TDLN, with this proliferation being inhibited by the PD-1/PD-L1 complex and CTLA-4.
elements.sent-2411-105: CD4+ and CD8+ T cells that have completed proliferation migrate to the TS and perform effector functions including the secretion of pro-inflammatory (IL-2, IFN-$\upgamma$, TNF, IL-12, IL-1$\upbeta$) and immunosuppressive (TGF-$\upbeta$, IL-10, IL-4, IL-6) cytokines.
elements.sent-2411-106: Extended exposure to the cancer antigen can lead CD8+ T cells to become exhausted, however, this exhaustion can be reversed by pembrolizumab.
elements.sent-2411-107: In addition, mature DCs, NK cells, neutrophils, macrophages and CAFs secrete cytokines that can activate NK cells, and polarise and repolarise macrophages and neutrophils into pro-inflammatory and immunosuppressive phenotypes.
elements.sent-2411-108: Cancer cells are killed by effector CD8+ T cells \cite{Raskov2020 , activated NK cells \cite{Zhang2020 and Th1 cells \cite{Knutson2005, Haabeth2014 through direct contact, whilst TNF and IFN-$\upgamma$ indirectly eliminate cancer cells via activating cell death pathways \cite{Josephs2018, Wang2008, Jorgovanovic2020 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: In particular, TNF and IFN-$\upgamma$ induce the necroptosis, programmed necrotic cell death, of cancer cells \cite{Wang2008, Castro2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-110: We note that TGF-$\upbeta$ and PD-L1 have been shown to inhibit cancer cell lysis by CD8+ T cells \cite{Thomas2005, Juneja2017 , and IL-6 and PD-1 have been shown to inhibit NK cell cytotoxicity \cite{Kang2014, Hsu2018 .}}
elements.sent-2411-111: We assume that cancer cells grow logistically, as is done in many CRC models \cite{Kirshtein2020, dePillis2013, Budithi2021 , due to space and resource competition in the TME.}

however, we employ Michaelis-Menten kinetics to accommodate for the limited rate of receptor recycling time \cite{Lai2017 .}}}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation} \frac{dC}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{C}C\left(1-\frac{C}{C_0}\right)}_{growth} - \underbrace{\lambda_{CT_8}T_8 \frac{1}{1+I_{\upbeta}/K_{CI_{\upbeta}}}\frac{1}{1+P_L/K_{CP_L}}C}_{\substack{elimination by $T_8$ \text{inhibited by $I_{\upbeta}$ and $P_{L}$}}} - \underbrace{\lambda_{CT_1}T_1C}_{elimination by $T_1$} - \underbrace{\lambda_{CK}K \frac{1}{1+I_{6}/K_{CI_{6}}}\frac{1}{1+P_D/K_{CP_D}}C}_{\substack{elimination by $K$ \text{inhibited by $I_{6}$ and $P_{D}$}}} - \underbrace{\lambda_{CI_{\upalpha}}\frac{I_{\upalpha}}{K_{CI_{\upalpha}}+I_{\upalpha}}C}_{\text{elimination by $I_{\upalpha}$}}- \underbrace{\lambda_{CI_{\upgamma}}\frac{I_{\upgamma}}{K_{CI_{\upgamma}}+I_{\upgamma}}C}_{\text{elimination by $I_{\upgamma}$}},  \label{cancereqn}  \frac{dN_c}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{CI_{\upalpha}}\frac{I_{\upalpha}}{K_{CI_{\upalpha}}+I_{\upalpha}}C}_{\text{Elimination by $I_{\upalpha}$}} + \underbrace{\lambda_{CI_{\upgamma}}\frac{I_{\upgamma}}{K_{CI_{\upgamma}}+I_{\upgamma}}C}_{\text{Elimination by $I_{\upgamma}$}} -\underbrace{d_{N_c}N_c}_{Removal}. \label{necroticcelleqn}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\frac{dH}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{HN_c} N_c}_{production by $N_c$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{HC_F}C_F}_{production by $C_F$}- \underbrace{d_{H} H}_{degradation}. \label{Heqn} \intertext{Necrotic cancer cells release ATP \cite{Martins2013} and calreticulin \cite{Ahmed2020} so that} \frac{dA}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{AN_c} N_c}_{production by $N_c$} - \underbrace{d_{A} A}_{degradation}, \label{Aeqn} \frac{dS}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{SN_c} N_c}_{production by $N_c$}- \underbrace{d_{S} S}_{degradation}. \label{Seqn}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-113: In addition, NK cells have been shown to efficiently kill immature DCs but not mature DCs \cite{Morandi2012, Vivier2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-114: Assuming that immature DCs are supplied at a rate $\mathcal{A _{D_0}$, we have that \begin{equation} \frac{dD_0}{dt} = \underbrace{A_{D_0}}_{source} - \underbrace{\lambda_{DH}D_0\frac{H}{K_{DH}+H}}_{$D_0 \to D$ by $H$} - \underbrace{\lambda_{DA}D_0\frac{A}{K_{DA}+A}}_{$D_0 \to D$ by $A$}- \underbrace{\lambda_{DS}D_0\frac{S}{K_{DS}+S}}_{$D_0 \to D$ by $S$}-\underbrace{\lambda_{D_0K} D_0K}_{elimination by $K$} - \underbrace{d_{D_0}D_0}_{death},  \label{D0eqn} \frac{dD}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{DH}D_0\frac{H}{K_{DH}+H}}_{$D_0 \to D$ by $H$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{DA}D_0\frac{A}{K_{DA}+A}}_{$D_0 \to D$ by $A$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{DS}D_0\frac{S}{K_{DS}+S}}_{$D_0 \to D$ by $S$} - \underbrace{\lambda_{DD^LN}D}_{\substack{$D$ migration to TDLN}} - \underbrace{d_{D}D}_{death}. \label{Deqn}\end{equation} We also need to consider that some mature DCs migrate into the T cell zone of the TDLN and stimulate naive CD8+ T cells, causing them to be activated, hence no longer naive \cite{Ruhland2020, Choi2017 .}}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation} \frac{dD_0}{dt} = \underbrace{A_{D_0}}_{source} - \underbrace{\lambda_{DH}D_0\frac{H}{K_{DH}+H}}_{$D_0 \to D$ by $H$} - \underbrace{\lambda_{DA}D_0\frac{A}{K_{DA}+A}}_{$D_0 \to D$ by $A$}- \underbrace{\lambda_{DS}D_0\frac{S}{K_{DS}+S}}_{$D_0 \to D$ by $S$}-\underbrace{\lambda_{D_0K} D_0K}_{elimination by $K$} - \underbrace{d_{D_0}D_0}_{death},  \label{D0eqn} \frac{dD}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{DH}D_0\frac{H}{K_{DH}+H}}_{$D_0 \to D$ by $H$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{DA}D_0\frac{A}{K_{DA}+A}}_{$D_0 \to D$ by $A$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{DS}D_0\frac{S}{K_{DS}+S}}_{$D_0 \to D$ by $S$} - \underbrace{\lambda_{DD^LN}D}_{\substack{$D$ migration to TDLN}} - \underbrace{d_{D}D}_{death}. \label{Deqn}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-115: We assume a fixed DC migration time of $\tau_m$ and also assume that only $e^{-d_{D}\tau_m}$ of the mature DCs that leave the TS survive migration.
elements.sent-2411-116: Taking into account the volume change between the TS site and the TDLN, we have that \begin{equation}\frac{dD^\mathrm{LN}}{dt} = \frac{V_\mathrm{TS}}{V_\mathrm{LN}}\underbrace{\lambda_{DD^\mathrm{LN}}e^{-d_D \tau_m}D(t-\tau_m)\theta(t-\tau_m)}_{\text{$D$ migration to TDLN}} - \underbrace{d_{D}D^\mathrm{LN}}_{\text{death}}, \label{DLNeqn}\end{equation} where $\theta(x)$ is the Heaviside function which equals $1$ if $x\geq 0$, and $0$ otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-117: The Heaviside function ensures positive invariance of the system by preventing cells from interacting before $t=0$.
elements.sent-2411-118: We assume that naive CD8+ T cells come into the TDLN at a constant rate and that they have not undergone cell division, nor will they until their activation.
elements.sent-2411-119: For simplicity, we do not consider cytokines in the TDLN, absorbing their influence into $\lambda_{T_0^8 T_A^8}$.
elements.sent-2411-120: We do, however, explicitly take into account the influence of PD-1 and CTLA-4 in the TDLN, which have been shown to inhibit T cell activation through limiting naive T cells from binding to mature DCs \cite{BrunnerWeinzierl2018, Mizuno2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-121: Thus, taking this all into account leads to \begin{equation}\frac{dT_0^8}{dt}=\underbrace{\mathcal{A}_{T_0^8}}_{\text{source}} -\underbrace{R^8(t)}_{\substack{\text{CD8+ T cell} \text{activation}}} - \underbrace{d_{T_0^8}T_0^8}_{\text{death}}, \label{naivecd8eqn}\end{equation} where $R^8(t)$ is defined as \begin{equation}R^8(t) := \underbrace{\frac{\lambda_{T_0^8 T_A^8}e^{-d_{T_0^8}\tau_8^\mathrm{act}}D^\mathrm{LN}(t-\tau_8^\mathrm{act})T_0^8(t-\tau_8^\mathrm{act})\theta(t-\tau_8^\mathrm{act})}{\left(1+\int_{t-\tau_8^\mathrm{act}}^{t} P^\mathrm{LN}_{D}(s) \ ds/K_{T_0^8P^\mathrm{LN}_{D}}\right)\left(1+\int_{t-\tau_8^\mathrm{act}}^{t} P^\mathrm{LN}_{A}(s) \ ds/K_{T_0^8P^\mathrm{LN}_{A}}\right)}}_{\text{CD8+ T cell activation inhibited by $P^\mathrm{LN}_{D}$ and $P^\mathrm{LN}_{A}$}}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}R^8(t) := \underbrace{\frac{\lambda_{T_0^8 T_A^8}e^{-d_{T_0^8}\tau_8^act}D^LN(t-\tau_8^act)T_0^8(t-\tau_8^act)\theta(t-\tau_8^act)}{\left(1+\int_{t-\tau_8^act}^{t} P^LN_{D}(s) \ ds/K_{T_0^8P^LN_{D}}\right)\left(1+\int_{t-\tau_8^act}^{t} P^LN_{A}(s) \ ds/K_{T_0^8P^LN_{A}}\right)}}_{\text{CD8+ T cell activation inhibited by $P^LN_{D}$ and $P^LN_{A}$}}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-122: In particular, since PD-1 and CTLA-4 inhibit T cell activation during the whole activation process, it is not sufficient to consider point estimates of PD-1 and CTLA-4 concentration.
elements.sent-2411-123: Instead, we resort to considering the integrals of the concentration of these immune checkpoint proteins throughout the entire $\tau_8^\mathrm{act}$ that the CD8+ T cell takes to complete activation.
elements.sent-2411-124: This is because these integrals are proportional (with a proportionality constant of $1/{\tau_8^\mathrm{act }$) to the average concentration of these proteins throughout activation, allowing us to properly incorporate its inhibition by PD-1 and CTLA-4.}
elements.sent-2411-125: We denote $d^8_n$ as the degradation rate of activated CD8+ T cells having undergone $n$ cell divisions.
elements.sent-2411-126: It is known that these activated CD8+ T cells undergo clonal expansion in the TDLN and differentiate before they stop proliferating and migrate to the TS \cite{Harris2002, Catron2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-127: For simplicity, we assume that the death rate of CD8+ T cells that have not completed their division program is equal to $d_{T_0^8}$, the death rate of naive CD8+ T cells, regardless of the number of cell divisions previously undergone.
elements.sent-2411-128: We also assume that only activated CD8+ T cells that have undergone $n^8_\mathrm{max}$ divisions will leave the TDLN and migrate to the TS.
elements.sent-2411-129: Furthermore, we assume a constant cell cycle time $\Delta_8$, except for the first cell division which has cycle time $\Delta_8^0$.
elements.sent-2411-130: Thus, the duration of the activated CD8+ T cell division program to $n^8_\mathrm{max}$ divisions is given by \begin{equation}\tau_{T_A^8} = \Delta_8^0 + (n^8_\mathrm{max}-1)\Delta_8.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-131: In particular, we must take into account that some T cells will die before the division program is complete, and so we must introduce a shrinkage factor of $e^{-d_{T_0^8}\tau_{T_A^8 }$.}
elements.sent-2411-132: Furthermore, we must also take into account that the PD-1/PD-L1 complex and CTLA-4 inhibit CD8+ T cell proliferation throughout the program \cite{Riley2009, Buchbinder2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-133: We finally assume that the death rate of CD8+ T cells that have completed their division program is equal to the death rate of CD8+ T cells in the TS.
elements.sent-2411-134: Taking this all into account leads to \begin{equation}\begin{split} \frac{dT_A^8}{dt} = \underbrace{\frac{2^{n^8_\mathrm{max}}e^{-d_{T_0^8}\tau_{T_A^8}}R^8(t- \tau_{T_A^8})}{\left(1+\int_{t- \tau_{T_A^8}}^{t} Q^\mathrm{LN}(s) \ ds/K_{T_A^8 Q^\mathrm{LN}}\right)\left(1+\int_{t - \tau_{T_A^8}}^{t} P^\mathrm{LN}_{A}(s) \ ds/K_{T_A^8P^\mathrm{LN}_{A}}\right)}}_{\text{CD8+ T cell proliferation inhibited by $P^\mathrm{LN}_{D}$ and $P^\mathrm{LN}_{A}$}} - \underbrace{\lambda_{T_A^8T_8}T_A^8}_{\substack{\text{$T_A^8$ migration} \text{to TS}}}-\underbrace{d_{T_8} T^8_A}_\text{death}. \end{split}\label{TA8n8maxeqn}\end{equation} One thing we must consider is that these CD8+ T cells must first migrate to the TS before they are able to attack and kill cancer cells.
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation} \frac{dT_A^8}{dt} = \underbrace{\frac{2^{n^8_max}e^{-d_{T_0^8}\tau_{T_A^8}}R^8(t- \tau_{T_A^8})}{\left(1+\int_{t- \tau_{T_A^8}}^{t} Q^LN(s) \ ds/K_{T_A^8 Q^LN}\right)\left(1+\int_{t - \tau_{T_A^8}}^{t} P^LN_{A}(s) \ ds/K_{T_A^8P^LN_{A}}\right)}}_{\text{CD8+ T cell proliferation inhibited by $P^LN_{D}$ and $P^LN_{A}$}} - \underbrace{\lambda_{T_A^8T_8}T_A^8}_{\substack{$T_A^8$ migration to TS}}-\underbrace{d_{T_8} T^8_A}_death. \label{TA8n8maxeqn}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-135: Importantly, only activated CD8+ T cells that have undergone $n^8_\mathrm{max}$ divisions will leave the TDLN and migrate to the TS.
elements.sent-2411-136: We denote $T_8(t)$ as the concentration of activated CD8+ T cells in the TS, and we assume that it takes $\tau_a$ amount of time for these cells to migrate to the TS.
elements.sent-2411-137: We must also account for CTL expansion due to positive growth factors such as IL-2 \cite{Rosenberg2014 , with this being inhibited by PD-1/PD-L1 complexes \cite{Jiang2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-138: Furthermore, the death of CD8+ T cells is resisted by IL-10 \cite{Oft2019, Qiu2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-139: However, chronic antigen exposure can cause effector CD8+ T cells to enter a state of exhaustion, where they lose their ability to kill cancer cells, and the rate of cytokine secretion significantly decreases \cite{Blank2019, Lee2016, Shive2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-140: We denote this exhausted CTL population as $T_\mathrm{ex (t)$.}
elements.sent-2411-141: It has also been shown that pembrolizumab can ``reinvigorate'' $T_\mathrm{ex}$ cells back into the effector state \cite{Pauken2015, Lee2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-142: We model the re-invigoration and exhaustion using Michaelis-Menten terms in $A_1$ and $\int_{t-\tau_{l ^t C(s) \ ds$ respectively, where $\tau_l$ is the median time that CD8+ T cells take to become exhausted after entering the TS.}}
elements.sent-2411-143: In particular, this has been shown to be more appropriate than simple mass-action kinetics as it accounts for extended antigen exposure \cite{DeBoer1995 .}
elements.sent-2411-144: As before, we introduce a Heaviside function $\theta(t-\tau_l)$, as we assume that CD8+ T cells need to spend at least $\tau_l$ in the TS for them to be able to be exhausted.
elements.sent-2411-145: For simplicity, we consider only Th1 and Th2 subtypes that naive CD4+ T cells differentiate into upon activation, absorbing the influence of cytokines via the kinetic rate constants $\lambda_{T_0^4 T_A^1}$ and $\lambda_{T_0^4 T_A^2}$ respectively.
elements.sent-2411-146: Taking into account that PD-1 and CTLA-4 inhibit CD4+ T cell activation and some mature DCs migrate into the TDLN and activate naive CD4+ T cells, causing them to no longer be naive, and assuming that naive CD4+ T cells come into the TDLN at a rate $\mathcal{A _{T_0^4}$, we can write a similar equation to \eqref{naivecd8eqn : \begin{equation}\frac{dT_0^4}{dt} =\underbrace{\mathcal{A}_{T_0^4}}_{\text{source}} - \underbrace{\left(R^1(t) + R^2(t) + R^r(t)\right)}_{\text{CD4+ T cell activation}} - \underbrace{d_{T_0^4}T_0^4}_{\text{death}}, \label{naivecd4eqn}\end{equation} where $R^i(t)$, for $i=1,2$, is defined as, \begin{equation}R^i(t) = \underbrace{\frac{\lambda_{T_0^4 T_A^i}e^{-d_{T_0^4}\tau_{\mathrm{act}}^4} D^\mathrm{LN}(t-\tau_{\mathrm{act}}^4)T_0^4(t-\tau_{\mathrm{act}}^4)\theta(t-\tau_{\mathrm{act}}^4)}{\left(1+\int_{t-\tau_4^\mathrm{act}}^{t} P_D^\mathrm{LN}(s) \ ds/K_{T_0^4 P^\mathrm{LN}_{D}}\right)\left(1+\int_{t-\tau_4^\mathrm{act}}^{t} P^\mathrm{LN}_{A}(s) \ ds/K_{T_0^4 P^\mathrm{LN}_{A}}\right)}}_{\text{$T_A^i$ activation inhibited by $P^\mathrm{LN}_{D}$ and $P^\mathrm{LN}_{A}$}}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation}R^i(t) = \underbrace{\frac{\lambda_{T_0^4 T_A^i}e^{-d_{T_0^4}\tau_{act}^4} D^LN(t-\tau_{act}^4)T_0^4(t-\tau_{act}^4)\theta(t-\tau_{act}^4)}{\left(1+\int_{t-\tau_4^act}^{t} P_D^LN(s) \ ds/K_{T_0^4 P^LN_{D}}\right)\left(1+\int_{t-\tau_4^act}^{t} P^LN_{A}(s) \ ds/K_{T_0^4 P^LN_{A}}\right)}}_{\text{$T_A^i$ activation inhibited by $P^LN_{D}$ and $P^LN_{A}$}}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-147: As before, we are interested in finding the concentration of activated CD4+ T cells over time, and for simplicity, we only consider the differentiation of CD4+ T cells into Th1 cells and Th2 cells.
elements.sent-2411-148: We assume that Th1 cells and Th2 cells proliferate up to $n^1_\mathrm{max}$ and $n^2_\mathrm{max}$ times, respectively, upon which they stop dividing.
elements.sent-2411-149: As before, we assume that the death rate of CD4+ T cells that have not completed their division program is equal to $d_{T_0^4}$, the death rate of naive CD4+ T cells, regardless of the number of cell divisions previously undergone or their phenotype.
elements.sent-2411-150: We assume a constant cell cycle time $\Delta_i$, except for the first cell division which has cycle time $\Delta_i^0$.
elements.sent-2411-151: Thus, the duration of the activated CD4+ T cell division program to $n^i_\mathrm{max}$ divisions is given by \begin{equation}\tau_{T_A^i} = \Delta_i^0 + (n^i_\mathrm{max}-1)\Delta_i.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-152: In particular, we must take into account that some T cells will die before the division program is complete, so we must introduce a shrinkage factor of $e^{-d_{T_0^4}\tau_{T_A^i }$.}

elements.sent-2411-154: We finally assume that the death rate of CD4+ T cells that have completed their division program is equal to the corresponding degradation rate in the TS.
elements.sent-2411-155: Taking this all into account, for $i=1,2$, leads to \begin{equation}\begin{split} \frac{dT_A^i}{dt} = \underbrace{\frac{2^{n^i_\mathrm{max}}e^{-d_{T_0^4}\tau_{T_A^i}} R^i(t- \tau_{T_A^i})}{\left(1+\int_{t- \tau_{T_A^i}}^{t} Q^\mathrm{LN}(s) \ ds/K_{T_A^i Q^\mathrm{LN}}\right)\left(1+\int_{t - \tau_{T_A^i}}^{t} P^\mathrm{LN}_{A}(s) \ ds/K_{T_A^iP^\mathrm{LN}_{A}}\right)}}_{\text{CD4+ T cell proliferation inhibited by $P^\mathrm{LN}_{D}$ and $P^\mathrm{LN}_{A}$}} - \underbrace{\lambda_{T_A^iT_i}T_A^i}_{\substack{\text{$T_A^i$ migration} \text{to TS}}}-\underbrace{d_{T_i} T^i_A}_\text{death}. \end{split}\label{TAinimaxeqn}\end{equation} We also assume that it takes $\tau_a$ amount of time for these cells to migrate to the TS.

elements.sent-2411-156: Hence, \begin{equation}\frac{dT_1}{dt} = \frac{V_LN}{V_TS}\underbrace{\lambda_{T_A^1T_1}e^{-d_{T_1} \tau_a}T_A^1(t-\tau_a)\theta(t-\tau_a)}_{$T_A^1$ migration to TS} +\underbrace{\lambda_{T_1 I_{2}}\frac{T_1 I_{2}}{K_{T_1 I_{2}}+ I_{2}}}_{growth by $I_2$}\underbrace{\frac{1}{1+Q/K_{T_1 Q}}}_{Inhibition by $Q$} - \underbrace{d_{T_1}T_1}_{death}, \label{th1eqn} \frac{dT_2}{dt} = \frac{V_LN}{V_TS}\underbrace{\lambda_{T_A^2T_2}e^{-d_{T_2} \tau_a}T_A^2(t-\tau_a)\theta(t-\tau_a)}_{$T_A^2$ migration to TS} - \underbrace{d_{T_2}T_2}_{death}. \label{th2eqn}\end{equation} Finally, we consider the concentration of Tregs in the TDLN, following the same procedure as for CD8+ T cells and CD4+ T helper cells.
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}\frac{dT_1}{dt} = \frac{V_LN}{V_TS}\underbrace{\lambda_{T_A^1T_1}e^{-d_{T_1} \tau_a}T_A^1(t-\tau_a)\theta(t-\tau_a)}_{$T_A^1$ migration to TS} +\underbrace{\lambda_{T_1 I_{2}}\frac{T_1 I_{2}}{K_{T_1 I_{2}}+ I_{2}}}_{growth by $I_2$}\underbrace{\frac{1}{1+Q/K_{T_1 Q}}}_{Inhibition by $Q$} - \underbrace{d_{T_1}T_1}_{death}, \label{th1eqn} \frac{dT_2}{dt} = \frac{V_LN}{V_TS}\underbrace{\lambda_{T_A^2T_2}e^{-d_{T_2} \tau_a}T_A^2(t-\tau_a)\theta(t-\tau_a)}_{$T_A^2$ migration to TS} - \underbrace{d_{T_2}T_2}_{death}. \label{th2eqn}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-157: We absorb the influence of cytokines on Treg activation via the kinetic rate constant $\lambda_{T_0^r T_A^r}$.

elements.sent-2411-159: Assuming that naive Tregs come into the TDLN at a rate $\mathcal{A _{T_0^r}$, we can write a similar equation to \eqref{naivecd8eqn and \eqref{naivecd4eqn : \begin{equation}\frac{dT_0^r}{dt} =\underbrace{\mathcal{A}_{T_0^r}}_{\text{source}} - \underbrace{R^r(t)}_{\text{Treg activation}} - \underbrace{d_{T_0^r}T_0^r}_{\text{death}}, \label{naivetregeqn}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}R^r(t) = \underbrace{\frac{\lambda_{T_0^r T_A^r}e^{-d_{T_0^r}\tau_{\mathrm{act}}^r} D^\mathrm{LN}(t-\tau_{\mathrm{act}}^r)T_0^r(t-\tau_{\mathrm{act}}^r)\theta(t-\tau_{\mathrm{act}}^r)}{\left(1+\int_{t-\tau_r^\mathrm{act}}^{t} P_D^\mathrm{LN}(s) \ ds/K_{T_0^r P^\mathrm{LN}_{D}}\right)\left(1+\int_{t-\tau_r^\mathrm{act}}^{t} P^\mathrm{LN}_{A}(s) \ ds/K_{T_0^r P^\mathrm{LN}_{A}}\right)}}_{\text{$T_A^r$ activation inhibited by $P^\mathrm{LN}_{D}$ and $P^\mathrm{LN}_{A}$}}.\end{equation}.}}}

elements.sent-2411-160: We assume that activated Tregs proliferate up to $n^r_\mathrm{max}$, times upon which they stop dividing.

elements.sent-2411-167: Taking this all into account leads to \begin{equation}\begin{split} \frac{dT_A^r}{dt} = \underbrace{\frac{2^{n^r_\mathrm{max}}e^{-d_{T_0^r}\tau_{T_A^r}} R^r(t- \tau_{T_A^r})}{\left(1+\int_{t- \tau_{T_A^r}}^{t} Q^\mathrm{LN}(s) \ ds/K_{T_A^r Q^\mathrm{LN}}\right)\left(1+\int_{t - \tau_{T_A^r}}^{t} P^\mathrm{LN}_{A}(s) \ ds/K_{T_A^rP^\mathrm{LN}_{A}}\right)}}_{\text{Treg proliferation inhibited by $P^\mathrm{LN}_{D}$ and $P^\mathrm{LN}_{A}$}} - \underbrace{\lambda_{T_A^rT_r}T_A^r}_{\substack{\text{$T_A^r$ migration} \text{to TS}}}-\underbrace{d_{T_r} T^r_A}_\text{death}. \end{split}\label{TArnrmaxeqn}\end{equation} Assuming that it also takes $\tau_a$ amount of time for Tregs to migrate to the TS, we have that \begin{equation}\frac{dT_r}{dt} = \frac{V_\mathrm{LN}}{V_\mathrm{TS}}\underbrace{\lambda_{T_A^rT_r}e^{-d_{T_r} \tau_a}T_A^r(t-\tau_a)\theta(t-\tau_a)}_{\text{$T_A^r$ migration to TS}} - \underbrace{d_{T_r}T_r}_{\text{death}}. \label{tregeqn}\end{equation} We assume that CAFs grow logistically for the same reason as cancer cells.

elements.sent-2411-175: In addition, immature and mature DCs activate NK cells \cite{Ferlazzo2002 .}
elements.sent-2411-176: Thus, assuming a supply rate $\mathcal{A _{K_0}$ of naive NK cells, we have that \begin{equation} \frac{dK_0}{dt} = \underbrace{A_{K_0}}_{source} - \underbrace{\lambda_{KI_2}K_0\frac{I_2}{K_{KI_2}+I_2}}_{$K_0 \to K$ by $I_2$} - \underbrace{\lambda_{KI_{12}}K_0\frac{I_{12}}{K_{KI_{12}}+I_{12}}}_{\text{$K_0 \to K$ by $I_{12}$}}-\underbrace{\lambda_{KD_{0}}K_0\frac{D_{0}}{K_{KD_0}+D_0}}_{$K_0 \to K$ by $D_0$}-\underbrace{\lambda_{KD}K_0\frac{D}{K_{KD}+D}}_{$K_0 \to K$ by $D$} - \underbrace{d_{K_0}K_0}_{degradation},  \label{K0eqn} \frac{dK}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{KI_2}K_0\frac{I_2}{K_{KI_2}+I_2}}_{$K_0 \to K$ by $I_2$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{KI_{12}}K_0\frac{I_{12}}{K_{KI_{12}}+I_{12}}}_{\text{$K_0 \to K$ by $I_{12}$}} +\underbrace{\lambda_{KD_{0}}K_0\frac{D_{0}}{K_{KD_0}+D_0}}_{$K_0 \to K$ by $D_0$}+\underbrace{\lambda_{KD}K_0\frac{D}{K_{KD}+D}}_{$K_0 \to K$ by $D$} - \underbrace{d_{K}K}_{degradation}. \label{Keqn}\end{equation} IL-2 is produced by Th1 cells \cite{Hwang2005 and CD8+ T cells \cite{Kish2005,DSouza2004 so that \begin{equation}\frac{dI_2}{dt} =\underbrace{\lambda_{I_2 T_1}T_1}_{production by $T_1$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_2 T_8}T_8}_{production by $T_8$} - \underbrace{d_{I_2}I_2}_{degradation}. \label{il2eqn} \intertext{IFN-$\upgamma$ is produced by Th1 cells \cite{Szabo2002, Castro2018} and CD8+ T cells \cite{Bhat2017}, with both expressions being inhibited by Tregs \cite{Sojka2011}. Activated NK cells also produce IFN-$\upgamma$ \cite{Cui2019}; however, this is inhibited by CTLA-4 \cite{Stojanovic2014}. Thus,} \frac{dI_{\upgamma}}{dt} =\left(\underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upgamma T_1}}T_1}_{production by $T_1$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upgamma} T_8}T_8}_{production by $T_8$}\right)\underbrace{\frac{1}{1+T_r/K_{I_{\upgamma}T_r}}}_{inhibition by $T_r$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upgamma K}}\frac{K}{1+P_A/K_{I_{\upgamma}P_A}}}_{\substack{production by $K$ inhibition by $P_A$}} - \underbrace{d_{I_\upgamma}I_\upgamma}_{degradation}. \label{ifngammaeqn} \intertext{TNF is produced by Th1 cells \cite{Basu2021, Zhang2014}, CD8+ T cells \cite{Hoekstra2021, Mehta2018}, M1 macrophages \cite{Chen2023}, N1 neutrophils \cite{Mihaila2021}, and activated NK cells \cite{Fauriat2010, Wang2011}. Hence,} \frac{dI_\upalpha}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upalpha}T_1}T_1}_{production by $T_1$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upalpha}T_8}T_8}_{production by $T_8$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upalpha}M_1}M_1}_{production by $M_1$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upalpha}N_1}N_1}_{production by $N_1$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upalpha}K}K}_{production by $K$}- \underbrace{d_{I_{\upalpha}}I_{\upalpha}}_{degradation}. \label{tnfeqn} \intertext{TGF-$\upbeta$ is produced by cancer cells \cite{Massagu2008}, Tregs \cite{Tang2008} and CAFs \cite{Wu2021}. Thus,} \frac{dI_\upbeta}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upbeta}C}C}_{production by $C$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upbeta}T_r}T_r}_{production by $T_r$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upbeta}C_F}C_F}_{production by $C_F$} - \underbrace{d_{I_{\upbeta}}I_{\upbeta}}_{degradation}. \label{tgfbetaeqn} \intertext{IL-12 is produced by N1 neutrophils \cite{Shaul2016,Mihaila2021} and M1 macrophages with IL-10 suppressing this expression of IL-12 \cite{Zheng2013, Rahim2005, deWaalMalefyt1991}. Additionally, mature DCs also produce IL-12; however, extracellular ATP has been shown to inhibit this production \cite{laSala2001}. Taking all of these factors into account, we have} \frac{dI_{12}}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{12}D}\frac{D}{1+A/K_{I_{12}A}}}_{\substack{production by $D$ inhibited by $A$}} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{12}N_1}N_1}_{production by $N_1$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{12}M_1}\frac{M_1}{1 + I_{10}/K_{I_{12}I_{10}}}}_{\substack{\text{production by $M_{1}$} \text{inhibited by $I_{10}$}}} - \underbrace{d_{I_{12}}I_{12}}_{degradation}. \label{il12eqn} \intertext{IL-10 is produced by Th2 cells \cite{del1993human, Fiorentino1989}, M2 macrophages \cite{Chen2019, Qi2016}, and cancer cells \cite{Itakura2011, KrgerKrasagakes1994}. Additionally, Tregs secrete IL-10 \cite{Moore2001} with IL-2 enhancing this production \cite{TsujiTakayama2008}. In particular, IL-2 binds to its cognate receptors and is rapidly internalised \cite{Malek2010} which we account for using Michaelis-Menten kinetics. Hence,} \frac{dI_{10}}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{10}C}C}_{production by $C$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{10}T_2}T_2}_{production by $T_2$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{10}M_2}M_2}_{production by $M_2$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{10}T_{r}}T_r\left(1+\lambda_{I_{10}I_{2}}\frac{I_{2}}{K_{I_{10}I_{2}}+I_{2}}\right)}_{production by $T_r$ enhanced by $I_2$} - \underbrace{d_{I_{10}}I_{10}}_{degradation}. \label{il10eqn} \intertext{IL-4 is produced by Th2 cells \cite{Gadani2012} so} \frac{dI_4}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{{I_4}{T_2}}T_2}_{production by $T_2$} - \underbrace{d_{I_4} I_4}_Degradation. \label{il4eqn} \intertext{IL-6 is produced by mature DCs \cite{Xu2021} and CAFs \cite{Wu2017}. As such,} \frac{dI_6}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{I_6 D} D}_{production by $D$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_6 C_F}C_F}_{production by $C_F$} - \underbrace{d_{I_6}I_6}_{degradation}. \label{il6eqn} \intertext{IL-1$\upbeta$ is produced by N1 neutrophils \cite{SansoresEspaa2022}, M1 macrophages \cite{Orecchioni2019}, and DCs \cite{Ainscough2014, Dinarello2009}. Thus, } \frac{dI_{1\upbeta}}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{1\upbeta}N_1}N_1}_{production by $N_1$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{1\upbeta}M_1}M_1}_{production by $M_1$}+ \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{1\upbeta}D}D}_{production by $D$} - \underbrace{d_{I_{1\upbeta}}I_{1\upbeta}}_{degradation}. \label{il1beqn}\end{equation} It is known that PD-1 is expressed on the surface of activated Th1 cells \cite{LuzCrawford2012 , activated CD8+ T cells \cite{Saito2012, Wu2014, Jiang2015 , activated Tregs \cite{Gianchecchi2018 , and M2 macrophages \cite{Kono2020, Gordon2017 .}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-022: \begin{equation} \frac{dK_0}{dt} = \underbrace{A_{K_0}}_{source} - \underbrace{\lambda_{KI_2}K_0\frac{I_2}{K_{KI_2}+I_2}}_{$K_0 \to K$ by $I_2$} - \underbrace{\lambda_{KI_{12}}K_0\frac{I_{12}}{K_{KI_{12}}+I_{12}}}_{\text{$K_0 \to K$ by $I_{12}$}}-\underbrace{\lambda_{KD_{0}}K_0\frac{D_{0}}{K_{KD_0}+D_0}}_{$K_0 \to K$ by $D_0$}-\underbrace{\lambda_{KD}K_0\frac{D}{K_{KD}+D}}_{$K_0 \to K$ by $D$} - \underbrace{d_{K_0}K_0}_{degradation},  \label{K0eqn} \frac{dK}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{KI_2}K_0\frac{I_2}{K_{KI_2}+I_2}}_{$K_0 \to K$ by $I_2$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{KI_{12}}K_0\frac{I_{12}}{K_{KI_{12}}+I_{12}}}_{\text{$K_0 \to K$ by $I_{12}$}} +\underbrace{\lambda_{KD_{0}}K_0\frac{D_{0}}{K_{KD_0}+D_0}}_{$K_0 \to K$ by $D_0$}+\underbrace{\lambda_{KD}K_0\frac{D}{K_{KD}+D}}_{$K_0 \to K$ by $D$} - \underbrace{d_{K}K}_{degradation}. \label{Keqn}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-023: \begin{equation}\frac{dI_2}{dt} =\underbrace{\lambda_{I_2 T_1}T_1}_{production by $T_1$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_2 T_8}T_8}_{production by $T_8$} - \underbrace{d_{I_2}I_2}_{degradation}. \label{il2eqn} \intertext{IFN-$\upgamma$ is produced by Th1 cells \cite{Szabo2002, Castro2018} and CD8+ T cells \cite{Bhat2017}, with both expressions being inhibited by Tregs \cite{Sojka2011}. Activated NK cells also produce IFN-$\upgamma$ \cite{Cui2019}; however, this is inhibited by CTLA-4 \cite{Stojanovic2014}. Thus,} \frac{dI_{\upgamma}}{dt} =\left(\underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upgamma T_1}}T_1}_{production by $T_1$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upgamma} T_8}T_8}_{production by $T_8$}\right)\underbrace{\frac{1}{1+T_r/K_{I_{\upgamma}T_r}}}_{inhibition by $T_r$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upgamma K}}\frac{K}{1+P_A/K_{I_{\upgamma}P_A}}}_{\substack{production by $K$ inhibition by $P_A$}} - \underbrace{d_{I_\upgamma}I_\upgamma}_{degradation}. \label{ifngammaeqn} \intertext{TNF is produced by Th1 cells \cite{Basu2021, Zhang2014}, CD8+ T cells \cite{Hoekstra2021, Mehta2018}, M1 macrophages \cite{Chen2023}, N1 neutrophils \cite{Mihaila2021}, and activated NK cells \cite{Fauriat2010, Wang2011}. Hence,} \frac{dI_\upalpha}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upalpha}T_1}T_1}_{production by $T_1$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upalpha}T_8}T_8}_{production by $T_8$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upalpha}M_1}M_1}_{production by $M_1$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upalpha}N_1}N_1}_{production by $N_1$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upalpha}K}K}_{production by $K$}- \underbrace{d_{I_{\upalpha}}I_{\upalpha}}_{degradation}. \label{tnfeqn} \intertext{TGF-$\upbeta$ is produced by cancer cells \cite{Massagu2008}, Tregs \cite{Tang2008} and CAFs \cite{Wu2021}. Thus,} \frac{dI_\upbeta}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upbeta}C}C}_{production by $C$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upbeta}T_r}T_r}_{production by $T_r$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upbeta}C_F}C_F}_{production by $C_F$} - \underbrace{d_{I_{\upbeta}}I_{\upbeta}}_{degradation}. \label{tgfbetaeqn} \intertext{IL-12 is produced by N1 neutrophils \cite{Shaul2016,Mihaila2021} and M1 macrophages with IL-10 suppressing this expression of IL-12 \cite{Zheng2013, Rahim2005, deWaalMalefyt1991}. Additionally, mature DCs also produce IL-12; however, extracellular ATP has been shown to inhibit this production \cite{laSala2001}. Taking all of these factors into account, we have} \frac{dI_{12}}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{12}D}\frac{D}{1+A/K_{I_{12}A}}}_{\substack{production by $D$ inhibited by $A$}} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{12}N_1}N_1}_{production by $N_1$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{12}M_1}\frac{M_1}{1 + I_{10}/K_{I_{12}I_{10}}}}_{\substack{\text{production by $M_{1}$} \text{inhibited by $I_{10}$}}} - \underbrace{d_{I_{12}}I_{12}}_{degradation}. \label{il12eqn} \intertext{IL-10 is produced by Th2 cells \cite{del1993human, Fiorentino1989}, M2 macrophages \cite{Chen2019, Qi2016}, and cancer cells \cite{Itakura2011, KrgerKrasagakes1994}. Additionally, Tregs secrete IL-10 \cite{Moore2001} with IL-2 enhancing this production \cite{TsujiTakayama2008}. In particular, IL-2 binds to its cognate receptors and is rapidly internalised \cite{Malek2010} which we account for using Michaelis-Menten kinetics. Hence,} \frac{dI_{10}}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{10}C}C}_{production by $C$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{10}T_2}T_2}_{production by $T_2$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{10}M_2}M_2}_{production by $M_2$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{10}T_{r}}T_r\left(1+\lambda_{I_{10}I_{2}}\frac{I_{2}}{K_{I_{10}I_{2}}+I_{2}}\right)}_{production by $T_r$ enhanced by $I_2$} - \underbrace{d_{I_{10}}I_{10}}_{degradation}. \label{il10eqn} \intertext{IL-4 is produced by Th2 cells \cite{Gadani2012} so} \frac{dI_4}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{{I_4}{T_2}}T_2}_{production by $T_2$} - \underbrace{d_{I_4} I_4}_Degradation. \label{il4eqn} \intertext{IL-6 is produced by mature DCs \cite{Xu2021} and CAFs \cite{Wu2017}. As such,} \frac{dI_6}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{I_6 D} D}_{production by $D$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_6 C_F}C_F}_{production by $C_F$} - \underbrace{d_{I_6}I_6}_{degradation}. \label{il6eqn} \intertext{IL-1$\upbeta$ is produced by N1 neutrophils \cite{SansoresEspaa2022}, M1 macrophages \cite{Orecchioni2019}, and DCs \cite{Ainscough2014, Dinarello2009}. Thus, } \frac{dI_{1\upbeta}}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{1\upbeta}N_1}N_1}_{production by $N_1$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{1\upbeta}M_1}M_1}_{production by $M_1$}+ \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{1\upbeta}D}D}_{production by $D$} - \underbrace{d_{I_{1\upbeta}}I_{1\upbeta}}_{degradation}. \label{il1beqn}\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-187: The equation for $P_L$ has a similar form to \eqref{PDeqn , except there is no anti-PD-L1 present, so that \begin{equation}P_{L} = \underbrace{\rho_{P_{L}^{C}}C + \rho_{P_{L}^{D}}D + \rho_{P_{L}^{C_F}}C_F + \rho_{P_{L}^{\mathrm{M2}}}M_2 + \rho_{P_{L}^{\mathrm{N1}}}N_1 + \rho_{P_{L}^{\mathrm{N2}}}N_2 + \rho_{P_{L}^{1}}T_1 + \rho_{P_{L}^{8}}T_8 + \rho_{P_{L}^{r}}T_r}_{\text{surface expression}}, \label{PLeqn}\end{equation} and \begin{equation}P_L^\mathrm{LN} = \underbrace{\rho_{P_{L}^{1}}T_A^1 + \rho_{P_{L}^{8}}T_A^8 + \rho_{P_{L}^{r}}T_A^r}_{\text{surface expression}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-188: PD-L1 binds to PD-1 on the surfaces of activated Th1 cells, activated CD8+ cells, Tregs and M2 macrophages forming the PD-1/PD-L1 complex \cite{Lin2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-189: This formation is a reversible chemical process, with association occurring at a rate of $\alpha_{P_{D P_{L }$ and disassociation occurring at a rate of $d_{Q}$, so that the reaction can be expressed as $P_{D +P_{L \underset{d_{Q {\stackrel{\alpha_{P_{D P_{L {\rightleftharpoons Q$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-190: Hence, \begin{equation}\frac{dQ}{dt} =\underbrace{\alpha_{P_{D}P_{L}}P_{D}P_{L}}_{formation} - \underbrace{d_{Q}Q}_{disassociation}, \intertext{Since the half-life of $Q$ is very small, being less than 1 second ($\approx 1.16 \times 10^{-5}$ days) \cite{Maute2015} , we employ a quasi-steady state approximation for $Q$, so that $\frac{dQ}{dt}=0 \implies \alpha_{P_{D}P_{L}}P_{D}P_{L} - d_{Q}Q$, or equivalently} Q = \frac{\alpha_{P_{D}P_{L}}}{d_Q} P_{D}P_{L}, \label{Qeqn} \intertext{and similarly,} Q^LN = \frac{\alpha_{P_{D} P_{L}}}{d_Q} P_{D}^LNP_{L}^LN. \label{QLNeqn}\end{equation} We assume that pembrolizumab is injected intravenously at times $t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n$ with doses $\gamma_{1 , \gamma_{2 , \dots, \gamma_{n}$ respectively.}}
elements.formula-2411-029: \begin{equation}\frac{dQ}{dt} =\underbrace{\alpha_{P_{D}P_{L}}P_{D}P_{L}}_{formation} - \underbrace{d_{Q}Q}_{disassociation}, \intertext{Since the half-life of $Q$ is very small, being less than 1 second ($\approx 1.16 \times 10^{-5}$ days) \cite{Maute2015} , we employ a quasi-steady state approximation for $Q$, so that $\frac{dQ}{dt}=0 \implies \alpha_{P_{D}P_{L}}P_{D}P_{L} - d_{Q}Q$, or equivalently} Q = \frac{\alpha_{P_{D}P_{L}}}{d_Q} P_{D}P_{L}, \label{Qeqn} \intertext{and similarly,} Q^LN = \frac{\alpha_{P_{D} P_{L}}}{d_Q} P_{D}^LNP_{L}^LN. \label{QLNeqn}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-191: Accounting for depletion due to blocking PD-1, the systemic nature of pembrolizumab distribution, and assuming that the duration of infusion is negligible in comparison to the time period of interest, we have that \begin{equation}\frac{dA_{1}}{dt} =\underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \gamma_{j}\delta\left(t-t_j\right)}_{infusion} - \underbrace{\frac{d_{P_{D}A_{1}} P_{D}A_{1}}{1+\int_0^t A_1(s) \ ds/K_{P_DA_1}}}_{\substack{depletion due to blocking PD-1 inhibited by $A_1$}} - \underbrace{d_{A_1}A_{1}}_{degradation}, \label{A1eqn} \intertext{and} \frac{dA_{1}^LN}{dt} =\underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \gamma_{j}\delta\left(t-t_j\right)}_{infusion} - \underbrace{\frac{d_{P_{D}^LN A_{1}^LN} P_{D}^LNA_{1}^LN}{1+\int_0^t A_1^LN(s) \ ds/K_{P_D^LN A_1^LN}}}_{\substack{depletion due to blocking PD-1 inhibited by $A_1^LN$}} - \underbrace{d_{A_1}A_{1}^LN}_{degradation}. \label{A1LNeqn}\end{equation} We now consider CTLA-4.
elements.formula-2411-030: \begin{equation}\frac{dA_{1}}{dt} =\underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \gamma_{j}\delta\left(t-t_j\right)}_{infusion} - \underbrace{\frac{d_{P_{D}A_{1}} P_{D}A_{1}}{1+\int_0^t A_1(s) \ ds/K_{P_DA_1}}}_{\substack{depletion due to blocking PD-1 inhibited by $A_1$}} - \underbrace{d_{A_1}A_{1}}_{degradation}, \label{A1eqn} \intertext{and} \frac{dA_{1}^LN}{dt} =\underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \gamma_{j}\delta\left(t-t_j\right)}_{infusion} - \underbrace{\frac{d_{P_{D}^LN A_{1}^LN} P_{D}^LNA_{1}^LN}{1+\int_0^t A_1^LN(s) \ ds/K_{P_D^LN A_1^LN}}}_{\substack{depletion due to blocking PD-1 inhibited by $A_1^LN$}} - \underbrace{d_{A_1}A_{1}^LN}_{degradation}. \label{A1LNeqn}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-192: We know that CTLA-4 is expressed on the surface of activated Th1 cells and Th2 cells \cite{Alegre1998 , activated CD8+ T cells \cite{Rowshanravan2018, Ha2018 , and activated Tregs \cite{Jain2010, Takahashi2000 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-193: However, it was found in \cite{Alegre1998 that the surface expression of CTLA-4 by Th1 cells is almost undetectable, whereas Th2 cell CTLA-4 surface expression was easily detected.}
elements.sent-2411-194: Thus, we do not consider CTLA-4 expression by Th1 cells.
elements.sent-2411-195: The equations for CTLA-4 are identical in structure to those for PD-L1, so that \begin{equation}P_A = \underbrace{\rho_{P_A^2}T_2 + \rho_{P_A^8}T_8 + \rho_{P_A^\mathrm{ex}}T_\mathrm{ex}+\rho_{P_A^r}T_r}_{\text{surface expression}}. \label{PAeqn}\end{equation} We also assume that the number of CTLA-4 molecules on T cells in the TDLN is the same as for those in the TS regardless of the number of cell divisions, and so we have that \begin{equation}\frac{dP^\mathrm{LN}_{A}}{dt} = \underbrace{ \rho_{P_A^8}T_A^8+\rho_{P_A^1}T_A^2+\rho_{P_A^r}T_A^r}_{\text{surface expression}}. \label{PALNeqn}\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-197: TDLN denotes the tumour-draining lymph node, whilst TS denotes the tumour site.. est.
elements.section-2411-003: Steady States and Initial Conditions \label{initcond section}
elements.sent-2411-198: We estimate all initial conditions and steady states under the assumption that no pembrolizumab has/will be administered.
elements.sent-2411-199: We assume that the patient initially has stage IIIA or stage IIIB MSI-H/dMMR colorectal adenocarcinoma with metastasis to at least one lymph node.
elements.subsection-2411-005: Tumour Site Cell Steady States and Initial Conditions
elements.sent-2411-200: Digital cytometry has proved itself to be a powerful technique in characterising immune cell populations from individual patients' bulk tissue transcriptomes without requiring physical cell isolation \cite{Newman2019, Le2020, Gong2011, Liebner2013, Newman2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-201: In particular, RNA-sequencing (RNA-seq) deconvolution of tumour gene expressions has been very useful in determining their immune profile and adjusting treatment accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-202: Corresponding clinical and biospecimen data were downloaded from the GDC portal \cite{Grossman2016 which includes tumour dimensions, necrotic cell percentage, AJCC TNM stage, and MSIsensor and MANTIS MSI statuses.}
elements.sent-2411-203: We filtered for samples from primary samples and with non-empty necrosis percentage data, from patients with AJCC stage III or stage IV CRC, and at least one of MANTIS score $>0.4$ or MSIsensor score $>3.5\%$, as these are the default thresholds for MSI-H \cite{Kautto2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-204: We use the stage IIIC and stage IV samples to infer steady states, and the stage IIIA and stage IIIB samples to infer initial conditions.
elements.sent-2411-205: For all algorithms outlined in the sequel, we aggregate the estimates by taking the median of the relevant non-zero values elementwise, and then normalising such that their sums become $1$.
elements.sent-2411-206: We first use the ImmuCellAI algorithm \cite{Miao2020 which estimates the abundance of 24 immune cell types from gene expression data and has also been shown to be highly accurate in predicting immunotherapy response.}
elements.sent-2411-207: These immune cell types include 18 T cell subsets, including CD4+ T cells which incorporate T helper cells (namely Th1 cells, Th2 cells, Th17 cells, and T follicular helper cells), regulatory T cells (including natural Tregs (nTregs), induced Tregs (iTregs), and type 1 regulatory T cells (Tr1s)), naive CD4+ T cells (CD4\_naive) and other CD4+ T cells (CD4\_T).
elements.sent-2411-208: In addition, they include naive CD8+ T cells (CD8\_T), cytotoxic T cells or CTLs (Tc), Tex cells, central memory T cells (Tcm), effector memory T cells (Tem), natural killer T cells (NKT), $\gamma\delta$ T cells (Tgd), and mucosal-associated invariant T cells (MAIT).
elements.sent-2411-209: ImmuCellAI also estimates the abundance of DCs, B cells, monocytes, macrophages, NK cells, and neutrophils.
elements.sent-2411-210: Direct correspondences between state variables in the model and ImmuCellAI cell types are shown in \Cref{ImmuCellmap .}
elements.sent-2411-211: Additionally, the aggregated estimated cell proportions generated by ImmuCellAI for steady states and initial conditions, after normalisation, are shown in \Cref{ImmuCellAIsteady and \Cref{ImmuCellAIinit .}}
elements.sent-2411-212: We followed a similar approach to \cite{Kirshtein2020 and \cite{Budithi2021 and applied CIBERSORTx B-mode on the refined gene expression data, using the validated LM22 signature matrix \cite{Newman2015 , which gave relative immune cell proportions of 22 immune cell types using 547 signature genes derived from microarray data.}}}
elements.sent-2411-213: Direct correspondences between state variables in the model and keys of the LM22 signature matrix are shown in \Cref{LM22map .}
elements.sent-2411-214: The aggregated estimated cell proportions generated by CIBERSORTx for steady states and initial conditions, after normalisation, are shown in \Cref{CIBERsteady and \Cref{CIBERinitialcond .}}
elements.sent-2411-215: In studies of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, the ratio of the median number of tumour-associated N1 and tumour-associated N2 neutrophils was $10.7:20\text{ -- 21$ \cite{Chen2022, Yin2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-216: From this, we assume that there is a $1:2$ ratio of N1/N2 neutrophils in locally advanced MSI-H/dMMR CRC.
elements.sent-2411-217: We also assume that the ratio of N0 neutrophils to the total of N1 and N2 neutrophils is equal to the ratio of M0 macrophages to the total of M1 and M2 macrophages.
elements.sent-2411-218: That is, at steady state, $N_0:N_1+N_2 = 0.048864 : 0.281657$, and we set the initial condition such that $N_0:N_1+N_2 = 0.062173 : 0.161427$.
elements.sent-2411-219: We also note that the density of immune cells in a healthy adult colon is approximately $3.37 \times 10^{7}\mathrm{~cell/g}$ \cite{Sender2023 , which assuming a tissue density of $1.03 \mathrm{~g/cm^3}$, results in a total immune cell density of $3.47 \times 10^{7}\mathrm{~cell/cm^3}$.}
elements.sent-2411-220: However, advanced cancer induces lymphadenopathy \cite{Lymphadenopathybook, West2016 , which \cite{Sender2023 estimates results in an increase in the total number of lymphocytes of at most $10\%$.}}
elements.sent-2411-221: As such, we assume that there is a $10\%$ increase in lymphocyte concentration in locally advanced MSI-H/dMMR CRC.
elements.sent-2411-222: MCP-counter uses a gene expression matrix to estimate the sample relative abundance of eight immune and two stromal cell populations in the TME from gene expression data and is amongst the highest accuracy and specificity estimation methods available \cite{Sturm2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-223: The aggregated estimated cell proportions generated by MCP-counter for steady states and initial conditions, after normalisation, are shown in \Cref{MCPsteady and \Cref{MCPinitialcond .}}
elements.sent-2411-224: Combining everything, the resultant steady-state approximations and initial conditions for the model are shown in \Cref{firstmodelsteady and \Cref{firstmodelinitcond .}}

All values are in $\mathrm{cell/cm^3}$.}
elements.sent-2411-225: We follow \cite{Budithi2021 , and at steady state assume that the density of cancer cells is approximately double that of the total number of immune cells (excluding fibroblasts).}
elements.sent-2411-226: From the TCGA biospecimen data, the median necrotic cancer cell percentage for stage IIIA and stage IIIB MSI-H CRC samples is $10\%$, and $5\%$ for stage IIIC and stage IV MSI-H CRC.
elements.sent-2411-227: As such, denoting $\mathrm{TIC}$ as the total immune cell density, $\mathrm{TCD}$ as the total cell density, and $N_p$ as the necrotic cell percentage, we have that \begin{equation}C + N_c = 2\times TIC, \frac{N_c}{N_p} =\frac{C}{1-N_p}, \quad C = 2\times TIC\times (1-N_p) \implies N_c =2\times TIC \times N_p.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-033: \begin{equation}C + N_c = 2\times TIC, \frac{N_c}{N_p} =\frac{C}{1-N_p}, \quad C = 2\times TIC\times (1-N_p) \implies N_c =2\times TIC \times N_p.\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-251: We now aim to optimise neoadjuvant pembrolizumab therapy in locally advanced MSI-H/dMMR CRC.
elements.sent-2411-252: For simplicity, we assume that pembrolizumab is given at a constant dosage and the spacing between consecutive pembrolizumab infusions is constant.
elements.sent-2411-253: We also assume that the patient has pembrolizumab at $t=0 \mathrm{~days}$, and we consider a treatment regimen lasting for $12$ weeks so that the time for the latest allowed infusion is $t=84 \mathrm{~days}$, and simulate to $20 \mathrm{~weeks = 140 \mathrm{~days}$.}
elements.sent-2411-254: We assume linear pharmacokinetics, so that $\xi_{j}$, $\gamma_{j = f_\mathrm{pembro}\xi_{j}$ for some scaling factor $f_\mathrm{pembro}$.}
elements.sent-2411-255: To determine these, we use the formula \begin{equation}f_pembro = \frac{C_max,ss(\xi_pembro)-C_min,ss(\xi_pembro)}{\xi_pembro}, \label{fA1pembroeqn}\end{equation} where $C_\mathrm{max,ss / C_\mathrm{min,ss}$ corresponds to the maximum and minimum serum concentration of pembrolizumab at steady state after a dose, $\xi_\mathrm{pembro}$, of pembrolizumab is administered, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-256: This results in $f_\mathrm{pembro \approx 2.90 \times 10^{-7 ~\mathrm{g/cm^3 \ \mathrm{day^{-1 /\mathrm{mg}$ of pembrolizumab administered for all doses.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-257: We define the efficacy at $t$ from this regimen to be \begin{equation}\operatorname{efficacy}(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},t) := \frac{V(t) - V(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},t)}{V(t)} \times 100\%.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-258: We also define the TVR similarly, as \begin{equation}\operatorname{TVR}(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},t) := \frac{V(0) - V(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},t)}{V(0)} \times 100\%.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-259: In particular, the efficacy represents the extent of tumour volume shrinkage throughout its growth course in comparison to no treatment, whereas the TVR reveals how much the absolute tumour volume has reduced since the commencement of treatment.
elements.sent-2411-260: We see that the TVR and efficacy are linearly related via the formula \begin{equation}\operatorname{TVR}(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},t) = \left(1 - \frac{V(0)}{V(t)} + \frac{V(0)\operatorname{efficacy}\left(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},t\right)}{V(t)}\right) \times 100\%, \label{tvreff}\end{equation} so that an increase in treatment efficacy results in increased TVR, and vice-versa.
elements.sent-2411-261: As such, we calculate only the TVR and the corresponding treatment efficacy can be calculated via \eqref{tvreff .}
elements.sent-2411-262: This corresponds to the ratio between the TVR percentage and the total dose of pembrolizumab administered.
elements.sent-2411-263: Experiments show that dosages of pembrolizumab between $0.1\mathrm{~mg/kg}$ and $10\mathrm{~mg/kg}$, given every 2 weeks, is safe and tolerable \cite{Robert2015, Chatterjee2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-264: We thus assume that the threshold for pembrolizumab toxicity is $10\mathrm{~mg/kg}$ every 2 weeks, with higher doses being deemed toxic.
elements.sent-2411-265: To rigorise this notion, we define the toxicity of the treatment regimen, with dose interval $\eta_\mathrm{pembro}$ and dosage $\xi_\mathrm{pembro}$, as \begin{equation}\operatorname{toxicity}(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},t) := \max\left( \frac{\underset{s \in [0,t]}{\max} \, A_1(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},s)}{\underset{s \in [0,t]}{\max} \, A_1(800 \mathrm{~mg};14\mathrm{~days},s)}, \frac{\underset{s \in [0,t]}{\max} \, A_1^\mathrm{LN}(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},s)}{\underset{s \in [0,t]}{\max} \, A_1^\mathrm{LN}(800 \mathrm{~mg};14\mathrm{~days},s)} \right),\end{equation} where $A_1(\xi_\mathrm{pembro ;\eta_\mathrm{pembro ,s), A_1^\mathrm{LN (\xi_\mathrm{pembro ;\eta_\mathrm{pembro ,s)$ denotes the concentration of $A_1$ and $A_1^\mathrm{LN}$ at time $s$ with pembrolizumab doses of $\xi_\mathrm{pembro}$ at a dosing interval of $\eta_\mathrm{pembro}$ respectively, and we assume a patient mass of $80\mathrm{~kg}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-266: In particular, the toxicity quantifies the ratio of the maximum pembrolizumab concentrations from the regimen to those of a $10\mathrm{~mg/kg}$ dose given every 2 weeks, taking the highest value of this ratio between the TDLN and TS.
elements.sent-2411-267: A toxicity greater than 1 indicates a toxic and unsafe regimen, whereas a toxicity of 1 or less signifies a non-toxic and safe regimen, with lower toxicity values corresponding to safer treatments.
elements.sent-2411-268: These values are integer factors of 84 days, and each $\eta_\mathrm{pembro}$ corresponds to a distinct number of doses administered.
elements.sent-2411-269: This approach ensures practicality whilst preventing any artefacts that could occur from selecting a treatment regimen that ends at a fixed time of 12 weeks.
elements.sent-2411-270: We consider $500$ linearly spaced dosages in the domain $\xi_\mathrm{pembro \in [1\mathrm{~mg/kg}$, $10\mathrm{~mg/kg ]$, corresponding to $\xi_\mathrm{pembro \in [80\mathrm{~mg , 800\mathrm{~mg ]$, assuming a patient mass of $80 \mathrm{~kg}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-271: There are 2 distinct treatment regimens for pembrolizumab therapy that are approved by the FDA for metastatic MSI-H/dMMR CRC in adults \cite{fda_pembrolizumab :.}
elements.sent-2411-272: Treatment 1: 200mg of pembrolizumab administered by intravenous infusion over a duration of $30$ minutes every 3 weeks until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity.
elements.sent-2411-273: Treatment 2: 400mg of pembrolizumab administered by intravenous infusion over a duration of $30$ minutes every 6 weeks until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity.
elements.sent-2411-274: These correspond to the following parameter values in the model:.
elements.sent-2411-277: Furthermore, this corresponds to a dose of $2.5\mathrm{~mg/kg (5\mathrm{~mg/kg )$ of pembrolizumab being administered in Treatment 1(2), assuming a patient mass of $\mathrm{~kg}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-278: All simulations were done in MATLAB using the dde23 solver with the initial conditions stated in \Cref{initcond section and drug parameters as above.}
elements.figure-2411-008: [H] \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{pembroplots.png} \caption{\label{pembroplots}TVR (top left and top right), efficiency (bottom left), and toxicity (bottom right) at $20$ weeks for $\eta_\mathrm{pembro}$ in the domain $\set{0,1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 12, 14, 21, 28, 42, 84}$ days with 500 linearly spaced $\xi_\mathrm{pembro}$ dosages between $80\mathrm{~mg}$ and $800\mathrm{~mg}$. The FDA-approved regimens (Treatment 1 and Treatment 2) for metastatic MSI-H/dMMR CRC are shown in black and the optimal treatment regimen for locally advanced MSI-H/dMMR CRC is shown in blue.}
elements.sent-2411-279: We can determine the optimal pembrolizumab therapy by considering the regimen that achieves an acceptable TVR at $20$ weeks, whilst minimising the total pembrolizumab administered as much as possible and ensuring a toxicity less than 1.
elements.sent-2411-280: The TVR of Treatment 1 at $20$ weeks was calculated to be $82.27\%$, and the TVR of Treatment 2 at $20$ weeks was calculated to be $87.35\%$.
elements.sent-2411-281: As such, to ensure that the TVR of the optimal treatment surpasses that of current FDA-approved pembrolizumab regimens, we set the threshold TVR to be $87.75\%$.
elements.sent-2411-282: We also consider constraints due to practicality, so that $\operatorname{eta ^\mathrm{opt _{\mathrm{pembro \in \{\mathrm{0,7,14,21,28,42,84}\} \mathrm{~days}$, so that the pembrolizumab dose intervals are an integer number of weeks.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-283: We also constrain that $\operatorname{eta ^\mathrm{opt _{\mathrm{pembro }$ is an integer multiple of $0.1\mathrm{~mg/kg}$, corresponding to an integer multiple of $8\mathrm{~mg}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-284: Denoting the space of $(\xi_\mathrm{pembro ,\eta_\mathrm{pembro )$ pairs that satisfy these criteria as $\mathcal{S ^\mathrm{prac}$, the optimal pembrolizumab dosing and spacing, denoted $\xi_\mathrm{pembro ^\mathrm{opt}$ and $\eta_\mathrm{pembro ^\mathrm{opt}$ satisfy \begin{equation}\left(\xi_\mathrm{pembro}^\mathrm{opt},\eta_\mathrm{pembro}^\mathrm{opt}\right) = \underset{\substack{\operatorname{TVR}(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},140) \geq 87.75\% (\xi_\mathrm{pembro},\eta_\mathrm{pembro}) \in \mathcal{S}^\mathrm{prac} \operatorname{toxicity}(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},140)\leq 1}}{\operatorname{argmax}} \ \operatorname{efficiency}\left(\xi_\mathrm{pembro}; \eta_\mathrm{pembro}, 140\right). \label{pembroopteqn}\end{equation} Solving \eqref{pembroopteqn , leads to the optimal therapy being $\left(\xi_\mathrm{pembro ^\mathrm{opt ,\eta_\mathrm{pembro ^\mathrm{opt}\right) = \left(760 \mathrm{~mg , 0 \mathrm{~days}\right)$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-285: This corresponds to a dose of $9.5 \mathrm{~mg/kg}$, and results in a TVR of $87.91\%$, an efficiency of $1.16 \times 10^{-1 \mathrm{\%/mg}$, and a toxicity of $3.41 \times 10^{-1}$ at $20$ weeks.}

Right: concentration of $C+N_c$, 20 weeks after pembrolizumab treatment commencement.}
