Time traces with no treatment are in black, Treatment 1 in red, Treatment 2 in blue, and the optimal pembrolizumab treatment regimen for locally advanced MSI-H/dMMR CRC in magenta.}
elements.sent-2411-288: We can see from \autoref{pembrooptplot and \autoref{comparisonoftreatment , that Treatments 1 and 2 are highly effective in eradicating cancer cells.}}
elements.sent-2411-289: Without treatment, the total cancer cell concentration (including necrotic cancer cells), at 20 weeks equals $7.34 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~cell/cm^3}$, however, equals $6.97 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~cell/cm^3}$, and $4.98 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~cell/cm^3}$ with Treatments 1 and 2 respectively.
elements.sent-2411-290: This corresponds to a $82.27\%$, and $87.35\%$ reduction in tumour volume by Treatments 1 and 2 respectively.
elements.sent-2411-291: In particular, this leads to two immediate, but important, observations.
elements.sent-2411-292: We must note that when comparing pembrolizumab, we must ensure that we take into account that locally advanced MSI-H/dMMR CRC patients will have not been treated with chemotherapy/other therapies and that these drugs are given as a first-line treatment.
elements.sent-2411-293: Firstly, higher doses at larger intervals are comparable but slightly more effective than smaller doses at shorter intervals, which is consistent with clinical and experimental observations for other cancers \cite{Simeone2020, DubPelletier2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-294: It is difficult to compare our results to that of pre-existing clinical trials for locally advanced MSI-H/dMMR CRC due to the lack of time-series data, widely varying treatment regimens tested and the broad range of outcomes found.
elements.sent-2411-295: Focusing on Treatment 1, which appears to be the primary focus of ongoing clinical trials, a tumour volume reduction of 82.27\% at 20 weeks, following the cessation of treatment at 12 weeks, is consistent with the extent of response observed.
elements.sent-2411-296: Therefore, we consider the model to be accurate, however; additional experimental data is needed for further verification.
elements.sent-2411-297: We also observe that slight variations in the initial conditions have minimal impact on model trajectories after a few days.
elements.sent-2411-298: Additionally, the first week of treatment induces transient behaviour, arising from the absence of interactions within the system for $t<0$.
elements.sent-2411-299: Moreover, it takes a couple of months for pembrolizumab to begin showing effects and for tumour reductions to be observed, in agreement with experimental findings.
elements.sent-2411-300: One of the most important observations is that with pembrolizumab, the concentration of effector CD8+ T cells increases greatly compared to without treatment, with the exhausted CD8+ T cell concentration decreasing correspondingly.
elements.sent-2411-301: In particular, the concentration of exhausted CD8+ T cells without treatment at 20 weeks is $1.67 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~cell/cm^3}$ compared to $1.10 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~cell/cm^3}$, $1.05 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~cell/cm^3}$, and $1.07 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~cell/cm^3}$ with Treatments 1 and 2, and the optimal treatment respectively.
elements.sent-2411-302: This showcases that a) pembrolizumab increases the concentration of cytotoxic CD8+ T cells through re-invigorating exhausted CD8+ T cells, and b) the concentration of exhausted CD8+ T cells plays a major role in treatment efficacy, as a reduction in exhausted T cells results in decreased PD-1 concentrations and improved cancer eradication.
elements.sent-2411-303: This occurs since the pembrolizumab concentration at the TS and TDLN gradually declines after the initial infusion so that reinvigoration of exhausted CD8+ T cells diminishes after a few months.
elements.sent-2411-304: However, the optimal therapy eradicates the tumour most rapidly, reducing the viable and necrotic cancer cell population to very low levels, eventually resulting in decreased DAMP release and a reduced influx of effector CD8+ T cells from the TDLN.
elements.sent-2411-305: Despite this, a sufficient number of effector CD8+ T cells and other cytotoxic cells remain to sufficiently kill the remaining cancer.
elements.sent-2411-306: In particular, this significantly reduced cancer population explains why the number of exhausted CD8+ T cells during the optimal treatment is comparable to Treatments 1 and 2, as the extent of antigen exposure is substantially decreased.
elements.sent-2411-307: The increased concentration of TNF is particularly noteworthy since it directly induces necroptosis of cancer cells, causing the release of DAMPs, which in turn induces DC maturation.
elements.sent-2411-308: Without treatment, TNF concentration at 20 weeks is $9.01 \times 10^{-8 \mathrm{~g/cm^3}$, compared to $9.72 \times 10^{-8 \mathrm{~g/cm^3}$, $9.74 \times 10^{-8 \mathrm{~g/cm^3}$, and $9.73 \times 10^{-8 \mathrm{~g/cm^3}$ with Treatments 1 and 2, and the optimal treatment respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-309: This further shows that increased TNF concentration is correlated with enhanced cancer death and treatment efficacy and that inducing more pro-inflammatory immunogenicity in the TME is key for this to occur.
elements.sent-2411-310: As expected, pembrolizumab therapy massively decreases the concentration of PD-1 in the TS and the TDLN.
elements.sent-2411-311: At 20 weeks, the concentration of PD-1 in the TS and the TDLN without treatment is $5.96 \times 10^9 \mathrm{~cell/cm^3}$ and $2.47 \times 10^{10 \mathrm{~cell/cm^3}$, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-312: However, with the optimal treatment, and with Treatments 1 and 2, at 20 weeks the concentration of PD-1 in the TS is reduced by 28.40\%, 26.25\%, and 28.53\% respectively, and its concentration in the TDLN reduces by 14.72\%, 12.63\%, and 14.17\% respectively.
elements.sent-2411-313: Thus, we see that treatment efficacy and success are directly correlated with the extent of PD-1 concentration reduction.
elements.sent-2411-314: This makes sense since PD-1 inhibits T cell activation in the TDLN, inhibits the cytotoxicity of NK cells, and interacts with PD-L1 to form PD-1/PD-L1 which inhibits T cell proliferation.
elements.sent-2411-315: Interestingly, after a couple of months of PD-1 blockade, CTLA-4 concentrations in the TS and TDLN decrease, enhancing T cell activation and proliferation in the TDLN.
elements.sent-2411-316: However, this also pertains to Tregs, resulting in their increased concentration in the TDLN and at the TS, and slightly reduced IFN-$\upgamma$ levels, highlighting the need to consider the balance between PD-1 inhibition and Treg proliferation.
elements.sent-2411-317: We see that TVR increases as the dosing increases and spacing decreases, noting that the effects of acquired resistance to pembrolizumab are quite small since the treatment regimen has a duration of only 12 weeks.
elements.sent-2411-318: In particular, the TVRs of Treatment 1 and Treatment 2 are high, and the TVR of treatments does not change significantly near these regions.
elements.sent-2411-319: Whilst these treatments are amongst the most efficient practical ones, they pale in comparison to the global maximum efficiency of $2.09 \times 10^{-1 \mathrm{\%/mg}$, achieved at $(\xi_\mathrm{pembro , \eta_\mathrm{pembro ) = (260 \mathrm{~mg , 0 \mathrm{~days )$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-320: There is also a clear transition between efficient and inefficient treatments, marked by the rapid shift in efficiency as one deviates from local optima.
elements.sent-2411-321: In particular, treatment is inefficient if its TVR is low, regardless of the dosing and spacing (corresponding to the top left inefficient region in \autoref{pembroplots ), or if an excessive amount of pembrolizumab is administered, regardless of the TVR (corresponding to the bottom right inefficient region in \autoref{pembroplots ).}}
elements.sent-2411-322: As expected, Treatments 1 and 2 are non-toxic, with toxicities of $1.85 \times 10^{-1}$ and $2.46 \times 10^{-1}$, respectively, whilst the optimal regimen has a toxicity of $3.41 \times 10^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-323: Thus, we see that these treatments are all non-toxic, and the optimal regimen has comparable toxicity to FDA-approved regimens for metastatic MSI-H/dMMR CRC.
elements.sent-2411-324: The optimal regimen of administering pembrolizumab as a single dose of $760 \mathrm{~mg}$ is also more efficient, more effective, leads to comparable or greater TVR, and is more cost-effective and convenient than current regimens, whilst again, maintaining practicality and safety.
elements.sent-2411-325: Moreover, lower single doses of pembrolizumab are effective, with a single dose of $400\mathrm{~mg}$ (equivalent to $5\mathrm{~mg/kg}$) achieving a TVR of $73.36\%$, and a single dose of $560\mathrm{~mg}$ (equivalent to $7\mathrm{~mg/kg}$) achieving a TVR of $84.03\%$.
elements.sent-2411-326: The associated toxicities are $1.79 \times 10^{-1}$ and $2.51 \times 10^{-1}$, respectively, which are lower than those of Treatments 1 and 2.
elements.sent-2411-327: Administering a single dose of pembrolizumab before surgery has proven highly effective for achieving long-term tumour eradication in a phase 1b clinical trial involving resectable stage III/IV melanoma \cite{Huang2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-328: In particular, 30\% of patients experienced $>90\%$ tumour eradication, and all of these patients remained disease-free at a median follow-up of 25 months.
elements.sent-2411-329: Medium to high single-dose pembrolizumab demonstrates promising potential for successful and cost-effective treatment, with a phase II clinical trial to evaluate the efficacy and safety of a single-dose of $4\mathrm{~mg/kg}$ of pembrolizumab in patients with stage I-III dMMR CRC currently underway \cite{Justesen2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-330: We ignored spatial effects in the model, however, their resolution can provide information about the distribution and clustering of different immune cell types in the TME and their clinical implications \cite{Barua2018, Maley2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-331: We assumed that the death rates were constant throughout the T cell proliferation program, however linear death rates, were shown to markedly improve the quality of fit of Deenick et al.'s model \cite{Deenick2003 to experimental data \cite{DeBoer2006 .}}
elements.sent-2411-332: We considered only the M1/M2 and N1/N2 macrophage and neutrophil dichotomies respectively, however, their plasticity motivates the description of their phenotypes as a continuum, giving them the ability to adapt their functions to achieve mixtures of M1/N1 and M2/N2 responses and functions \cite{Mills2012, Hsu2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-333: In the optimisation of neoadjuvant pembrolizumab therapy, we restricted ourselves to treatments with constant dosing and spacing as is common in the literature, however varying dosages and dosing frequencies may result in improved regimens.
elements.sent-2411-334: We did not consider T cell avidity, the overall strength of a TCR-pMHC interaction, which governs whether a cancer cell will be successfully killed \cite{Kumbhari2020n2 .}
elements.sent-2411-335: In particular, high-avidity T cells are necessary for lysing cancer cells and durable tumour eradication, whilst low-avidity T cells are ineffective, and may inhibit high-avidity T cells \cite{Kumbhari2021, Chung2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-336: We also did not consider the influence of cytokines in the TDLN for T cell activation and proliferation, which are important in influencing effector T cell differentiation \cite{Curtsinger1999, Raphael2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-337: The definition of toxicity does not account for its potential origins in autoimmunity, which is a crucial component of certain adverse effects \cite{Wang2018tox .}
elements.sent-2411-338: In this work, we have provided a framework for mathematically modelling many immune cell types in the TME, using experimental data to govern parameter estimation, and finally analysing and optimising neoadjuvant pembrolizumab therapy in MSI-H/dMMR CRC for efficacy and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-339: We conclude that medium to high single-dose pembrolizumab is more efficient and demonstrates comparable or greater efficacy and TVR than current FDA-approved regimens for metastatic MSI-H/dMMR CRC, whilst maintaining practicality and safety.
elements.sent-2411-340: In addition, the versatility and power of the methods and equations herein can be easily adapted to attain a more comprehensive understanding of other cancers and improve healthcare as a result.

elements.sent-2411-351: If no pembrolizumab is present, then $P_D$ simply follows the equation \begin{equation}P_D = \rho_{P_D^1}T_1 + \rho_{P_D^8}T_8 + \rho_{P_D^ex}T_ex+\rho_{P_D^r}T_r+\rho_{P_D^M2}M_2 =\rho_{P_D^1} \left(T_1 + \frac{\rho_{P_D^8}}{\rho_{P_D^1}}T_8 + \frac{\rho_{P_D^ex}}{\rho_{P_D^1}}T_ex+\frac{\rho_{P_D^r}}{\rho_{P_D^1}}T_r+\frac{\rho_{P_D^M2}}{\rho_{P_D^1}}M_2\right), \intertext{so that} \frac{dP_D}{dt} =\rho_{P_D^1}\left(\frac{dT_1}{dt} + \frac{\rho_{P_D^8}}{\rho_{P_D^1}}\frac{dT_8}{dt} + \frac{\rho_{P_D^ex}}{\rho_{P_D^1}}\frac{T_ex}{dt}+\frac{\rho_{P_D^r}}{\rho_{P_D^1}}\frac{dT_r}{dt}+\frac{\rho_{P_D^M2}}{\rho_{P_D^1}}\frac{dM_2}{dt}\right),\end{equation} where $\rho_{P_D^1}$ is constant.
elements.sent-2411-352: However, when pembrolizumab is injected, PD-1 on these PD-1 expressing cells can bind to it, causing their depletion \cite{han2020pdsupp and in turn changing the number of PD-1 molecules expressed on each cell.}
elements.sent-2411-353: Thus, we can replace $\rho_{P_D^1}$ above with $P_D/\left(T_1 + \frac{\rho_{P_D^8 {\rho_{P_D^1 T_8 + \frac{\rho_{P_D^\mathrm{ex {\rho_{P_D^1 T_\mathrm{ex +\frac{\rho_{P_D^r {\rho_{P_D^1 T_r+\frac{\rho_{P_D^\mathrm{M2 {\rho_{P_D^1 M_2\right)$ so that $dP_D/dt$ is given by, taking resistance and the depletion into account, \begin{equation}\frac{dP_D}{dt} = \frac{P_D}{T_1 + \frac{\rho_{P_D^8}}{\rho_{P_D^1}}T_8 + \frac{\rho_{P_D^ex}}{\rho_{P_D^1}}T_ex+\frac{\rho_{P_D^r}}{\rho_{P_D^1}}T_r+\frac{\rho_{P_D^M2}}{\rho_{P_D^1}}M_2}\left(\frac{dT_1}{dt} + \frac{\rho_{P_D^8}}{\rho_{P_D^1}}\frac{dT_8}{dt} + \frac{\rho_{P_D^ex}}{\rho_{P_D^1}}\frac{T_ex}{dt}+\frac{\rho_{P_D^r}}{\rho_{P_D^1}}\frac{dT_r}{dt}+\frac{\rho_{P_D^M2}}{\rho_{P_D^1}}\frac{dM_2}{dt}\right) \quad - \frac{d_{P_{D}A_{1}} P_{D}A_{1}}{1+\int_0^t A_1(s) \ ds/K_{P_DA_1}} =P_D\frac{\left(\rho_{P_D^1}\frac{dT_1}{dt} + \rho_{P_D^8}\frac{dT_8}{dt} + \rho_{P_D^ex}\frac{dT_ex}{dt}+ \rho_{P_D^r}\frac{dT_r}{dt} + \rho_{P_D^M2}\frac{dM_2}{dt}\right)}{\rho_{P_D^1}T_1 + \rho_{P_D^8}T_8 + \rho_{P_D^ex}T_ex+\rho_{P_D^r}T_r+\rho_{P_D^M2}M_2} - \frac{d_{P_{D}A_{1}} P_{D}A_{1}}{1+\int_0^t A_1(s) \ ds/K_{P_DA_1}},\end{equation} which is the same as \eqref{PDeqn .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-043: \begin{equation}\frac{dP_D}{dt} = \frac{P_D}{T_1 + \frac{\rho_{P_D^8}}{\rho_{P_D^1}}T_8 + \frac{\rho_{P_D^ex}}{\rho_{P_D^1}}T_ex+\frac{\rho_{P_D^r}}{\rho_{P_D^1}}T_r+\frac{\rho_{P_D^M2}}{\rho_{P_D^1}}M_2}\left(\frac{dT_1}{dt} + \frac{\rho_{P_D^8}}{\rho_{P_D^1}}\frac{dT_8}{dt} + \frac{\rho_{P_D^ex}}{\rho_{P_D^1}}\frac{T_ex}{dt}+\frac{\rho_{P_D^r}}{\rho_{P_D^1}}\frac{dT_r}{dt}+\frac{\rho_{P_D^M2}}{\rho_{P_D^1}}\frac{dM_2}{dt}\right) \quad - \frac{d_{P_{D}A_{1}} P_{D}A_{1}}{1+\int_0^t A_1(s) \ ds/K_{P_DA_1}} =P_D\frac{\left(\rho_{P_D^1}\frac{dT_1}{dt} + \rho_{P_D^8}\frac{dT_8}{dt} + \rho_{P_D^ex}\frac{dT_ex}{dt}+ \rho_{P_D^r}\frac{dT_r}{dt} + \rho_{P_D^M2}\frac{dM_2}{dt}\right)}{\rho_{P_D^1}T_1 + \rho_{P_D^8}T_8 + \rho_{P_D^ex}T_ex+\rho_{P_D^r}T_r+\rho_{P_D^M2}M_2} - \frac{d_{P_{D}A_{1}} P_{D}A_{1}}{1+\int_0^t A_1(s) \ ds/K_{P_DA_1}},\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-358: We estimate all parameters, where possible, under the assumption that no pembrolizumab has/will be administered.
elements.sent-2411-359: The exception to this is the parameters directly related to pembrolizumab treatment for which the assumptions are explicitly stated during estimation.
elements.sent-2411-360: We also denote $1 \mathrm{~d}$ to be $1$ day, $1 \mathrm{~h}$ to be $1$ hour, $1 \mathrm{~m}$ to be $1$ minute, and $1 \mathrm{~s}$ to be $1$ second.
elements.subsection-2411-011: DAMP Steady States and Initial Conditions \label{dampssinitappendix}
elements.sent-2411-361: Like cytokines, we note that $1 \mathrm{~cm^3 = 1 \mathrm{~mL}$ for all DAMP measurements.}
elements.sent-2411-362: In \cite{Zhang2019supp , a study of blood samples from 144 patients with CRC was conducted, with the serum HMGB1 levels of patients with distant metastasis being $13.32\pm 6.12\mathrm{~\mu g/L}$, which was significantly higher than those with only lymphatic metastasis at $10.14 \pm 4.38\mathrm{~\mu g/L}$.}
elements.sent-2411-363: We assume that the serum concentrations and tissue concentrations of HMGB1 are similar, so we take the initial condition of $H$ to be $1.014 \times 10^{-8 \mathrm{~g/cm^3}$ and the steady state to be $1.33 \times 10^{-8}\mathrm{~g/cm^3}$.}
elements.sent-2411-364: Extracellular ATP is present in high concentrations in the TME, and in the TME and metastases, extracellular ATP concentration can reach several hundred $\mathrm{\mu M}$ \cite{DiVirgilio2016supp .}
elements.sent-2411-365: In particular, concentrations of extracellular ATP in the TME range between $100$–$700 \mathrm{~\mu M}$ \cite{Pellegatti2008supp , which is $10^3\text{ -- 10^4$ times higher than in healthy tissue \cite{Cao2023supp .}}}
elements.sent-2411-366: Since extracellular ATP is produced by necrotic cancer cells, which have a larger population at steady state compared to initially, we assume that there is more extracellular ATP at steady state.
elements.sent-2411-367: As such, we take the initial condition for $A$ to be equal to $100\mathrm{~\mu M}$ and a steady state equal to $700\mathrm{~\mu M}$, corresponding to $6.02 \times 10^{16 \mathrm{~molec/cm^3}$ and $4.22 \times 10^{17 \mathrm{~molec/cm^3}$, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-368: Recalling that ATP has a molecular mass of approximately $507.181 \mathrm{~Da = 8.419 \times 10^{-22 \mathrm{~g}$ \cite{Ghosh2006supp , this corresponds to an initial condition of $5.06 \times 10^{-5}\mathrm{~g/cm^3}$ and a steady state of $3.55 \times 10^{-4 \mathrm{~g/cm^3}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-369: In epithelial ovarian cancer (EOC), calreticulin concentrations when no drugs are introduced were approximately $ 2 \times 10^{-2 \pm 2.5 \times 10^{-2 \mathrm{~\mu g /\mathrm{mL}$ \cite{Abdullah2021supp .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-370: Since surface calreticulin is produced by necrotic cancer cells, which have a larger population at steady state compared to initially, we assume that there is more surface calreticulin at steady state.
elements.sent-2411-371: We assume that calreticulin concentrations in EOC are similar to that of MSI-H/dMMR CRC, so we assume an initial condition for $S$ of $2 \times 10^{-8}\mathrm{~g/cm^3}$ and a steady state of $4.5 \times 10^{-8}\mathrm{~g/cm^3}$.
elements.subsection-2411-012: Cytokine Steady States and Initial Conditions \label{cytokinessinitappendix}
elements.sent-2411-372: To estimate cytokine steady states and initial conditions, we look at the respective experimental tissue concentration data, noting that this is more accurate than the more widely available serum/plasma concentration data.
elements.sent-2411-373: Nonetheless, we use serum/plasma concentration data, where relevant, to guide estimates if the corresponding tissue concentration data is limited.
elements.sent-2411-374: We note that $1 \mathrm{~cm^3 = 1 \mathrm{~mL}$ for all cytokine measurements.}
elements.sent-2411-375: The tissue concentration of IL-2 in CRC is very low and was found to be below the lower limit of quantification in various experiments \cite{Calu2021supp, kim2014associationsupp .}
elements.sent-2411-376: In tumour supernatants of invasive ductal cancer the median IL-2 concentration was found $2.1 \mathrm{~pg/mL}$ with the interquartile range being $2.0\mathrm{~pg/mL - 4.9\mathrm{~pg/mL}$ \cite{Autenshlyus2017supp .}}
elements.sent-2411-377: We assume similar concentrations of IL-2 in the tissue of CRC patients.
elements.sent-2411-378: It was found in \cite{Calu2021supp that the median tissue concentration of IFN-$\upgamma$ in CRC patients was $15.2 \mathrm{~pg/mL}$, with the upper quartile concentration being approximately $16.9 \mathrm{~pg/mL}$.}
elements.sent-2411-379: It was found in \cite{Sharp2017supp that the serum concentration of IFN-$\upgamma$ in stage IV CRC patients (median $\approx 20.75\mathrm{~pg/mL}$) is significantly higher than that of stage I-III patients (median $\approx 1\mathrm{~pg/mL}$).}
elements.sent-2411-380: We thus set the steady state of $I_\upgamma$ to $1.69 \times 10^{-8 \mathrm{~g/cm^3}$, with an initial condition of $1.52 \times 10^{-8 \mathrm{~g/cm^3}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-381: It was found in \cite{Sharp2017supp that in advanced CRC patients, i.e those with stage III or stage IV disease, the mean TNF tissue concentration was $\approx 53 \mathrm{~pg/mL}$, with the concentration one standard deviation above the mean being approximately $90 \mathrm{~pg/mL}$.}
elements.sent-2411-382: Furthermore, the serum TNF concentration in stage IV CRC patients (median $20.3\mathrm{~pg/mL}$) is significantly higher than in stage III CRC patients (median $16.0\mathrm{~pg/mL}$) \cite{KrzystekKorpacka2013supp .}
elements.sent-2411-383: We thus set the steady state of $I_\upalpha$ to $9.00 \times 10^{-8 \mathrm{~g/cm^3}$, with an initial condition of $5.30 \times 10^{-8 \mathrm{~g/cm^3}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-384: It was found in \cite{Wang2012supp that in advanced CRC patients, i.e those with stage III or stage IV disease, the mean TGF-$\upbeta$ tissue concentration was $1311.5 \mathrm{~pg/mg}$, with the concentration one standard deviation above the mean being $1469.1 \mathrm{~pg/mg}$.}
elements.sent-2411-385: Assuming a tissue density of $1.03 \mathrm{~g/mL}$, these correspond to tissue concentrations of $1.35 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~pg/mL}$ and $1.51 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~pg/mL}$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-386: Furthermore, the serum TGF-$\upbeta$ concentration in stage IV CRC patients (mean $55\mathrm{~pg/mL}$) is significantly higher than in stage III CRC patients (mean $45\mathrm{~pg/mL}$) \cite{Shim1999supp .}
elements.sent-2411-387: We thus set the steady state of $I_\upbeta$ to $1.51 \times 10^{-3 \mathrm{~g/cm^3}$, with an initial condition of $1.35 \times 10^{-3 \mathrm{~g/cm^3}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-388: In CRC cells in 3D spheroid cultures, the mean IL-12 concentration from cell supernatants was found to be $\approx 0.6\mathrm{~pg/mL}$, with the concentration one standard deviation above the mean being $\approx 0.625 \mathrm{~pg/mL}$ \cite{Bersano2023supp .}
elements.sent-2411-389: Furthermore, the serum IL-12 concentration in metastatic CRC patients is lower than in patients without distant metastasis \cite{CzajkaFrancuz2020supp .}

Thus,} \frac{dI_{\upgamma}}{dt} =\left(\underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upgamma} T_8}T_8}_{production by $T_8$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upgamma T_1}}T_1}_{production by $T_1$}\right)\underbrace{\frac{1}{1+T_r/K_{I_{\upgamma}T_r}}}_{inhibition by $T_r$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upgamma K}}\frac{K}{1+P_A/K_{I_{\upgamma}P_A}}}_{\substack{production by $K$ inhibition by $P_A$}} - \underbrace{d_{I_\upgamma}I_\upgamma}_{degradation}. \label{ifngammaeqn} \intertext{TNF is produced by CD8+ T cells \cite{Hoekstra2021, Mehta2018}, Th1 cells \cite{Basu2021, Zhang2014}, M1 macrophages \cite{Chen2023}, N1 neutrophils \cite{Mihaila2021}, and activated NK cells \cite{Fauriat2010, Wang2011}. Hence,} \frac{dI_\upalpha}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upalpha}T_8}T_8}_{production by $T_8$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upalpha}T_1}T_1}_{production by $T_1$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upalpha}M_1}M_1}_{production by $M_1$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upalpha}N_1}N_1}_{production by $N_1$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{\upalpha}K}K}_{production by $K$}- \underbrace{d_{I_{\upalpha}}I_{\upalpha}}_{degradation}. \label{tnfeqn} \intertext{TGF-$\upbeta$ is produced by cancer cells \cite{Massagu2008}, Tregs \cite{Tang2008} and CAFs \cite{Wu2021}. Additionally, Tregs secrete IL-10 \cite{Moore2001} with IL-2 enhancing this production \cite{TsujiTakayama2008}. Hence,} \frac{dI_{10}}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{10}C}C}_{production by $C$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{10}T_2}T_2}_{production by $T_2$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{10}M_2}M_2}_{production by $M_2$} + \underbrace{\lambda_{I_{10}T_{r}}T_r\left(1+\lambda_{I_{10}I_{2}}\frac{I_{2}}{K_{I_{10}I_{2}}+I_{2}}\right)}_{production by $T_r$ enhanced by $I_2$} - \underbrace{d_{I_{10}}I_{10}}_{degradation}. \label{il10eqn} \intertext{IL-4 is produced by Th2 cells \cite{Gadani2012} so} \frac{dI_4}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{{I_4}{T_2}}T_2}_{production by $T_2$} - \underbrace{d_{I_4} I_4}_Degradation. \label{il4eqn} \intertext{IL-6 is produced by mature DCs \cite{Xu2021} and CAFs \cite{Wu2017}. elements.sent-2411-620: The equation for $P_L$ has a similar form to \eqref{PDeqn , except there is no anti-PD-L1 present, so that \begin{equation}P_{L} = \underbrace{\rho_{P_{L}^{C}}C + \rho_{P_{L}^{D}}D + \rho_{P_{L}^{8}}T_8 + \rho_{P_{L}^{1}}T_1 + \rho_{P_{L}^{r}}T_r + \rho_{P_{L}^{C_F}}C_F + \rho_{P_{L}^{\mathrm{M2}}}M_2 + \rho_{P_{L}^{\mathrm{N1}}}N_1 + \rho_{P_{L}^{\mathrm{N2}}}N_2}_{\text{surface expression}}. \label{PLeqn}\end{equation} For simplicity, we do not consider PD-L1 on DCs in the TDLN, so that \begin{equation}P_L^\mathrm{LN} = \underbrace{\rho_{P_{L}^{8}}T_A^8 + \rho_{P_{L}^{1}}T_A^1 + \rho_{P_{L}^{r}}T_A^r}_{\text{surface expression}}.\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-623: Hence, \begin{equation}\frac{dQ}{dt} =\underbrace{\alpha_{P_{D}P_{L}}P_{D}P_{L}}_{formation} - \underbrace{d_{Q}Q}_{dissociation}. \intertext{Since the half-life of $Q$ is very small, being less than 1 second ($\approx 1.16 \times 10^{-5}$ days) \cite{Maute2015}, we employ a quasi-steady state approximation for $Q$, so that $\frac{dQ}{dt}=0 \implies \alpha_{P_{D}P_{L}}P_{D}P_{L} - d_{Q}Q$, or equivalently} Q = \frac{\alpha_{P_{D}P_{L}}}{d_Q} P_{D}P_{L}, \label{Qeqn} \intertext{and similarly,} Q^LN = \frac{\alpha_{P_{D} P_{L}}}{d_Q} P_{D}^LNP_{L}^LN. \label{QLNeqn}\end{equation} We assume that pembrolizumab is injected intravenously at times $t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n$ with doses $\gamma_{1 , \gamma_{2 , \dots, \gamma_{n}$ respectively.}}
elements.formula-2411-170: \begin{equation}\frac{dQ}{dt} =\underbrace{\alpha_{P_{D}P_{L}}P_{D}P_{L}}_{formation} - \underbrace{d_{Q}Q}_{dissociation}. \intertext{Since the half-life of $Q$ is very small, being less than 1 second ($\approx 1.16 \times 10^{-5}$ days) \cite{Maute2015}, we employ a quasi-steady state approximation for $Q$, so that $\frac{dQ}{dt}=0 \implies \alpha_{P_{D}P_{L}}P_{D}P_{L} - d_{Q}Q$, or equivalently} Q = \frac{\alpha_{P_{D}P_{L}}}{d_Q} P_{D}P_{L}, \label{Qeqn} \intertext{and similarly,} Q^LN = \frac{\alpha_{P_{D} P_{L}}}{d_Q} P_{D}^LNP_{L}^LN. \label{QLNeqn}\end{equation}

elements.figure-2411-023: [H] \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{pembroplots.png} \caption{\label{pembroplots}TVR (top left and top right), efficiency (bottom left), and toxicity (bottom right) at $20$ weeks for $\eta_\mathrm{pembro}$ in the domain $\set{1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 12, 14, 21, 28, 42, 84, \infty}$ days with 500 linearly spaced $\xi_\mathrm{pembro}$ dosages between $80\mathrm{~mg}$ and $800\mathrm{~mg}$. The FDA-approved regimens (Treatment 1 and Treatment 2) for metastatic MSI-H/dMMR CRC are shown in black and the optimal treatment regimen for locally advanced MSI-H/dMMR CRC is shown in blue. We note that the bounds represented by the colour bar of the top-right figure differ from those in the top-left figure.}
elements.sent-2411-630: We can determine the optimal pembrolizumab therapy by considering the regimen that achieves an acceptable TVR at $20$ weeks, whilst maximising treatment efficiency as much as possible and ensuring a toxicity of less than 1.

elements.sent-2411-786: Colorectal cancer (CRC) poses a major public health challenge due to its increasing prevalence, particularly among younger populations.
elements.sent-2411-787: Microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H) CRC and deficient mismatch repair (dMMR) CRC constitute 15\% of all CRC and exhibit remarkable responsiveness to immunotherapy, especially with PD-1 inhibitors.
elements.sent-2411-788: Despite this, there is a significant need to optimise immunotherapeutic regimens to maximise clinical efficacy and patient quality of life whilst minimising monetary costs.
elements.sent-2411-789: To address this, we employ a novel framework driven by delay integro-differential equations to model the interactions among cancer cells, immune cells, and immune checkpoints.
elements.sent-2411-790: Several of these components are being modelled deterministically for the first time in cancer, paving the way for a deeper understanding of the complex underlying immune dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-791: We consider two compartments: the tumour site and the tumour-draining lymph node, incorporating phenomena such as dendritic cell (DC) migration, T cell proliferation, and CD8+ T cell exhaustion and reinvigoration.
elements.sent-2411-792: Parameter values and initial conditions are derived from experimental data, integrating various pharmacokinetic, bioanalytical, and radiographic studies, along with deconvolution of bulk RNA-sequencing data from the TCGA COADREAD and GSE26571 datasets.
elements.sent-2411-793: We finally optimise neoadjuvant treatment with pembrolizumab, a widely used PD-1 inhibitor, to balance efficacy, efficiency, and toxicity in locally advanced MSI-H/dMMR CRC patients.
elements.sent-2411-794: We improve upon currently FDA-approved therapeutic regimens for metastatic MSI-H/dMMR CRC, demonstrating that a single medium-to-high dose of pembrolizumab is sufficient for effective tumour eradication whilst being efficient, safe and practical.
elements.sent-2411-795: Colorectal cancer (CRC) is the third most common cancer worldwide, accounting for approximately $10\%$ of all cancer cases \cite{Klimeck2023 with more than 1.85 million cases and 850,000 deaths annually \cite{Biller2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-796: In the United States, the 5-year survival rates for stage IIIA, stage IIIB, and Stage IIIC colon cancer are 90\%, 72\%, and 53\%, respectively, whilst stage IV CRC has a 5-year survival of only 12\% \cite{Rawla2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-797: Approximately 20\% of stage II, 12\% of stage III, and 4\% of stage IV CRC tumours are diagnosed as MSI-H/dMMR \cite{Sinicrope2011, Venderbosch2014, Fan2024 , with approximately 80\% of sporadic MSI-H/dMMR CRC caused by MLH1 promoter hypermethylation \cite{Andr2020 .}}
elements.sent-2411-798: PD-1, a cell membrane receptor that is expressed on a variety of cell types, including activated T cells, activated B cells and monocytes, has been extensively researched in the context of cancer such as MSI-H/dMMR CRC \cite{Sarshekeh2018, Yaghoubi2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-799: One such phase II study is the NEOPRISM-CRC, NCT05197322, 31 patients with a high TMR and high-risk stage II or stage III MSI-H/dMMR CRC were given three cycles of pembrolizumab, at a dose of 200 mg every 3 weeks via IV infusion, and underwent surgery 4 -- 6 weeks after the last dose was administered \cite{Shiu2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-800: As part of this, 27 MSI-H/dMMR CRC patients with locally advanced cancer were either given 200 mg pembrolizumab via IV infusion every 3 weeks for eight treatments followed by surgical resection or 200 mg pembrolizumab via IV infusion every 3 weeks for 16 treatments.
elements.sent-2411-801: Overall, 21 MSI-H/dMMR patients exhibited pCR, and among the 14 MSI-H/dMMR CRC patients in the resection group, 11 exhibited pCRs.
elements.sent-2411-802: Additionally, after a median follow-up time of 9.5 months, only two patients who underwent surgical resection experienced recurrence or progression.
elements.sent-2411-803: Kirshtein et al. developed an ordinary differential equation (ODE) model of CRC progression incorporating immunological components such as T helper cells, Tregs, dendritic cells (DCs), macrophages, and they considered the effects of carcinogenic cytokines and immunosuppressive agents \cite{Kirshtein2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-804: They used data from the TCGA COADREAD database \cite{COADREAD2012 to perform estimates for the steady states and initial conditions of model variables and considered data from all patients, regardless of the TNM stage.}
elements.sent-2411-805: Bozkurt et al. presented a relatively simple ODE model of CRC treatment with anthracycline doxorubicin and IL-2 immunotherapy, modelling cancer cells, natural killer (NK) cells, CD8+ T cells, and other lymphocytes \cite{Bozkurt2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-806: De Pillis et al. developed an ODE model of CRC with irinotecan and monoclonal antibody therapies, in particular cetuximab and panitumumab, modelling similar quantities \cite{dePillis2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-807: No papers to date have properly considered this inhibition deterministically throughout the whole proliferation and activation programs since examining inhibition at a single moment in time is insufficient to characterise this properly.

elements.sent-2411-810: In this work, we construct a mathematical model using data-driven delay integro-differential equations that addresses these drawbacks, incorporating all of the aforementioned processes and species, and use this to optimise neoadjuvant pembrolizumab therapy for locally advanced MSI-H/dMMR CRC.

elements.sent-2411-839: We do, however, explicitly take into account the influence of PD-1 in the TDLN, which has been shown to inhibit T cell activation through limiting naive T cells from binding to mature DCs \cite{BrunnerWeinzierl2018, Mizuno2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-840: Recalling that T cells that have become activated by mature DCs are no longer naive, and taking this all into account, leads to \begin{equation}\frac{dT_0^8}{dt}=\underbrace{\mathcal{A}_{T_0^8}}_{\text{source}} -\underbrace{R^8(t)}_{\substack{\text{CD8+ T cell} \text{activation}}} - \underbrace{d_{T_0^8}T_0^8}_{\text{death}}, \label{naivecd8eqn}\end{equation} where $R^8(t)$ is defined as \begin{equation}R^8(t) := \underbrace{\frac{\lambda_{T_0^8 T_A^8}e^{-d_{T_0^8}\tau_8^\mathrm{act}}D^\mathrm{LN}(t-\tau_8^\mathrm{act})T_0^8(t-\tau_8^\mathrm{act})}{1+\int_{t-\tau_8^\mathrm{act}}^{t} P^{8\mathrm{LN}}_{D}(s) \ ds/K_{T_0^8P^{8\mathrm{LN}}_{D}}}}_{\text{CD8+ T cell activation inhibited by $P^{8\mathrm{LN}}_{D}$}}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-218: \begin{equation}R^8(t) := \underbrace{\frac{\lambda_{T_0^8 T_A^8}e^{-d_{T_0^8}\tau_8^act}D^LN(t-\tau_8^act)T_0^8(t-\tau_8^act)}{1+\int_{t-\tau_8^act}^{t} P^{8LN}_{D}(s) \ ds/K_{T_0^8P^{8LN}_{D}}}}_{\text{CD8+ T cell activation inhibited by $P^{8LN}_{D}$}}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-841: In particular, since PD-1 inhibits T cell activation during the whole activation process, it is not sufficient to consider point estimates of PD-1 concentration.
elements.sent-2411-842: Instead, we resort to considering the integrals of the concentration of this immune checkpoint protein throughout the entire $\tau_8^\mathrm{act}$ time that the CD8+ T cell takes to complete activation.
elements.sent-2411-843: This is because these integrals are proportional (with a proportionality constant of $1/{\tau_8^\mathrm{act }$) to the average concentration of these proteins throughout activation, allowing us to properly incorporate its inhibition by PD-1.}
elements.subsubsection-2411-075: Equation for Effector CD8+ T Cells in the TDLN ($T_A^8$)
elements.sent-2411-844: We also assume that only activated CD8+ T cells that have undergone $n^8_\mathrm{max}$ divisions become effector CD8+ T cells, which will leave the TDLN and migrate to the TS.
elements.sent-2411-845: Furthermore, we assume a constant cell cycle time $\Delta_8$, except for the first cell division, which has cycle time $\Delta_8^0$.
elements.sent-2411-846: Furthermore, we must also take into account that the PD-1/PD-L1 complex inhibits CD8+ T cell proliferation throughout the program \cite{Riley2009, Buchbinder2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-847: We must also consider that some of these effector CD8+ T cells will migrate to the TS to perform effector functions.
elements.sent-2411-848: Taking this all into account leads to \begin{equation}\begin{split} \frac{dT_A^8}{dt} = \underbrace{\frac{2^{n^8_\mathrm{max}}e^{-d_{T_0^8}\tau_{T_A^8}}R^8(t- \tau_{T_A^8})}{1+\int_{t- \tau_{T_A^8}}^{t} Q^{8\mathrm{LN}}(s) \ ds/K_{T_A^8 Q^{8\mathrm{LN}}}}}_{\text{CD8+ T cell proliferation inhibited by $Q^{8\mathrm{LN}}$}} - \underbrace{\lambda_{T_A^8T_8}T_A^8}_{\substack{\text{$T_A^8$ migration} \text{to TS}}}-\underbrace{d_{T_8} T^8_A}_\text{death}. \end{split}\label{TA8n8maxeqn}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-219: \begin{equation} \frac{dT_A^8}{dt} = \underbrace{\frac{2^{n^8_max}e^{-d_{T_0^8}\tau_{T_A^8}}R^8(t- \tau_{T_A^8})}{1+\int_{t- \tau_{T_A^8}}^{t} Q^{8LN}(s) \ ds/K_{T_A^8 Q^{8LN}}}}_{\text{CD8+ T cell proliferation inhibited by $Q^{8LN}$}} - \underbrace{\lambda_{T_A^8T_8}T_A^8}_{\substack{$T_A^8$ migration to TS}}-\underbrace{d_{T_8} T^8_A}_death. \label{TA8n8maxeqn}\end{equation}
elements.subsubsection-2411-076: Equation for Effector and Exhausted CD8+ T Cells in the TS ($T_8$ and $T_\mathrm{ex}
elements.sent-2411-849: We must also account for CTL expansion due to positive growth factors such as IL-2 \cite{Rosenberg2014 .}
elements.subsubsection-2411-077: Equation for Naive CD4+ T Cells in the TDLN ($T_0^4$)
elements.sent-2411-850: For simplicity, we consider only the Th1 subtype that naive CD4+ T cells differentiate into upon activation, absorbing the influence of cytokines via the kinetic rate constant $\lambda_{T_0^4 T_A^1}$.
elements.sent-2411-851: Taking into account that PD-1 inhibits Th1 cell activation and some mature DCs migrate into the TDLN and activate naive CD4+ T cells, causing them to no longer be naive, and assuming that naive CD4+ T cells come into the TDLN at a rate $\mathcal{A _{T_0^4}$, we can write a similar equation to \eqref{naivecd8eqn : \begin{equation}\frac{dT_0^4}{dt} =\underbrace{\mathcal{A}_{T_0^4}}_{\text{source}} - \underbrace{\left(R^1(t)\right)}_{\text{CD4+ T cell activation}} - \underbrace{d_{T_0^4}T_0^4}_{\text{death}}, \label{naivecd4eqn}\end{equation} where $R^1(t)$ is defined as \begin{equation}R^1(t) = \underbrace{\frac{\lambda_{T_0^4 T_A^1}e^{-d_{T_0^4}\tau_{\mathrm{act}}^4} D^\mathrm{LN}(t-\tau_{\mathrm{act}}^4)T_0^4(t-\tau_{\mathrm{act}}^4)}{1+\int_{t-\tau_4^\mathrm{act}}^{t} P_D^{1\mathrm{LN}}(s) \ ds/K_{T_0^4 P^{1\mathrm{LN}}_{D}}}}_{\text{$T_A^1$ activation inhibited by $P^{1\mathrm{LN}}_{D}$}}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-221: \begin{equation}R^1(t) = \underbrace{\frac{\lambda_{T_0^4 T_A^1}e^{-d_{T_0^4}\tau_{act}^4} D^LN(t-\tau_{act}^4)T_0^4(t-\tau_{act}^4)}{1+\int_{t-\tau_4^act}^{t} P_D^{1LN}(s) \ ds/K_{T_0^4 P^{1LN}_{D}}}}_{\text{$T_A^1$ activation inhibited by $P^{1LN}_{D}$}}.\end{equation}
elements.subsubsection-2411-078: Equation for Effector Th1 Cells in the TDLN ($T_A^1$)
elements.sent-2411-852: We assume that Th1 cells proliferate up to $n^1_\mathrm{max}$ times, upon which they stop dividing and become effector cells.
elements.sent-2411-853: As before, we assume that the death rate of Th1 that have not completed their division program is equal to $d_{T_0^4}$, the death rate of naive CD4+ T cells, regardless of the number of cell divisions previously undergone or their phenotype.
elements.sent-2411-854: We assume a constant cell cycle time $\Delta_1$, except for the first cell division, which has cycle time $\Delta_1^0$.
elements.sent-2411-855: Thus, the duration of the Th1 cell division program to $n^1_\mathrm{max}$ divisions is given by \begin{equation}\tau_{T_A^1} = \Delta_1^0 + (n^1_\mathrm{max}-1)\Delta_1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-856: In particular, we must take into account that some Th1 cells will die before the division program is complete, so we must introduce a shrinkage factor of $e^{-d_{T_0^4}\tau_{T_A^1 }$.}
elements.sent-2411-857: Furthermore, we must also take into account that the PD-1/PD-L1 complex inhibits Th1 cell proliferation throughout its program \cite{Riley2009, Buchbinder2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-858: We also assume that the death rate of Th1 cells that have completed their division program is equal to the corresponding degradation rate in the TS.
elements.sent-2411-859: Taking this all into account, and incorporating effector Th1 cell migration to the TS, leads to \begin{equation}\frac{dT_A^1}{dt} = \underbrace{\frac{2^{n^1_\mathrm{max}}e^{-d_{T_0^4}\tau_{T_A^1}} R^1(t- \tau_{T_A^1})}{1+\int_{t- \tau_{T_A^1}}^{t} Q^{1\mathrm{LN}}(s) \ ds/K_{T_A^1 Q^{1\mathrm{LN}}}}}_{\text{Th1 cell proliferation inhibited by $Q^{1\mathrm{LN}}$}} - \underbrace{\lambda_{T_A^1T_1}T_A^1}_{\substack{\text{$T_A^1$ migration} \text{to TS}}}-\underbrace{d_{T_1} T^1_A}_\text{death}, \label{TA1n1maxeqn}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-223: \begin{equation}\frac{dT_A^1}{dt} = \underbrace{\frac{2^{n^1_max}e^{-d_{T_0^4}\tau_{T_A^1}} R^1(t- \tau_{T_A^1})}{1+\int_{t- \tau_{T_A^1}}^{t} Q^{1LN}(s) \ ds/K_{T_A^1 Q^{1LN}}}}_{\text{Th1 cell proliferation inhibited by $Q^{1LN}$}} - \underbrace{\lambda_{T_A^1T_1}T_A^1}_{\substack{$T_A^1$ migration to TS}}-\underbrace{d_{T_1} T^1_A}_death, \label{TA1n1maxeqn}.\end{equation}
elements.subsubsection-2411-079: Equation for Effector Th1 Cells in the TS ($T_1$)
elements.sent-2411-860: We assume that it takes $\tau_a$ amount of time for these cells to migrate to the TS.
elements.sent-2411-861: We take into account the fact that IL-2 induces the growth of effector Th1 cells \cite{Choudhry2018 .. Hence, \begin{equation}\frac{dT_1}{dt} = \frac{V_LN}{V_TS}\underbrace{\lambda_{T_A^1T_1}e^{-d_{T_1} \tau_a}T_A^1(t-\tau_a)}_{$T_A^1$ migration to TS} +\underbrace{\lambda_{T_1 I_{2}}\frac{T_1 I_{2}}{K_{T_1 I_{2}}+ I_{2}}}_{growth by $I_2$}- \underbrace{d_{T_1}T_1}_{death}, \label{th1eqn}.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-224: \begin{equation}\frac{dT_1}{dt} = \frac{V_LN}{V_TS}\underbrace{\lambda_{T_A^1T_1}e^{-d_{T_1} \tau_a}T_A^1(t-\tau_a)}_{$T_A^1$ migration to TS} +\underbrace{\lambda_{T_1 I_{2}}\frac{T_1 I_{2}}{K_{T_1 I_{2}}+ I_{2}}}_{growth by $I_2$}- \underbrace{d_{T_1}T_1}_{death}, \label{th1eqn}.\end{equation}
elements.subsubsection-2411-080: Equation for Naive Tregs in the TDLN ($T_0^r$)
elements.sent-2411-862: Finally, we consider the concentration of naive Tregs in the TDLN, following the same procedure as for CD8+ T cells and Th1 cells.
elements.sent-2411-863: We also take into account that PD-1 inhibits Treg activation, and some mature DCs migrate into the TDLN and activate naive Tregs, causing them to no longer be naive.
elements.sent-2411-864: Assuming that naive Tregs come into the TDLN at a rate $\mathcal{A _{T_0^r}$, we can write a similar equation to \eqref{naivecd8eqn and \eqref{naivecd4eqn : \begin{equation}\frac{dT_0^r}{dt} =\underbrace{\mathcal{A}_{T_0^r}}_{\text{source}} - \underbrace{R^r(t)}_{\text{Treg activation}} - \underbrace{d_{T_0^r}T_0^r}_{\text{death}}, \label{naivetregeqn}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}R^r(t) = \underbrace{\frac{\lambda_{T_0^r T_A^r}e^{-d_{T_0^r}\tau_{\mathrm{act}}^r} D^\mathrm{LN}(t-\tau_{\mathrm{act}}^r)T_0^r(t-\tau_{\mathrm{act}}^r)}{1+\int_{t-\tau_r^\mathrm{act}}^{t} P_D^{r\mathrm{LN}}(s) \ ds/K_{T_0^r P^{r\mathrm{LN}}_{D}}}}_{\text{$T_A^r$ activation inhibited by $P^{r\mathrm{LN}}_{D}$}}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.formula-2411-225: \begin{equation}R^r(t) = \underbrace{\frac{\lambda_{T_0^r T_A^r}e^{-d_{T_0^r}\tau_{act}^r} D^LN(t-\tau_{act}^r)T_0^r(t-\tau_{act}^r)}{1+\int_{t-\tau_r^act}^{t} P_D^{rLN}(s) \ ds/K_{T_0^r P^{rLN}_{D}}}}_{\text{$T_A^r$ activation inhibited by $P^{rLN}_{D}$}}.\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-867: Furthermore, we must also take into account that the PD-1/PD-L1 complex inhibits Treg proliferation throughout the program \cite{Marangoni2021, Riley2009 .}
elements.sent-2411-868: Taking this all into account, and incorporating effector Treg migration to the TS, leads to \begin{equation}\begin{split} \frac{dT_A^r}{dt} = \underbrace{\frac{2^{n^r_\mathrm{max}}e^{-d_{T_0^r}\tau_{T_A^r}} R^r(t- \tau_{T_A^r})}{1+\int_{t- \tau_{T_A^r}}^{t} Q^{r\mathrm{LN}}(s) \ ds/K_{T_A^r Q^{r\mathrm{LN}}}}}_{\text{Treg proliferation inhibited by $Q^{r\mathrm{LN}}$}} - \underbrace{\lambda_{T_A^rT_r}T_A^r}_{\substack{\text{$T_A^r$ migration} \text{to TS}}}-\underbrace{d_{T_r} T^r_A}_\text{death}. \end{split}\label{TArnrmaxeqn}\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-912: We used the stage IIIC samples to infer steady states and the stage IIIA and stage IIIB samples to infer initial conditions.

elements.sent-2411-939: We now aim to optimise neoadjuvant pembrolizumab therapy for locally advanced MSI-H/dMMR CRC.
elements.sent-2411-940: For simplicity, we assume that pembrolizumab is given at a constant dosage, and the spacing between consecutive pembrolizumab infusions is constant.
elements.sent-2411-941: We also assume that the patient has pembrolizumab at $t=0 \mathrm{~days}$, and we consider a treatment regimen lasting for $12$ weeks so that the time for the latest allowed infusion is $t=84 \mathrm{~days}$, and simulate to $24 \mathrm{~weeks = 168 \mathrm{~days}$.}
elements.sent-2411-942: In our optimisation of pembrolizumab therapy, we consider the following four endpoints: tumour concentration reduction (TCR), efficacy, efficiency, and toxicity.
elements.sent-2411-943: We define the TCR from this regimen to be \begin{equation}\operatorname{TCR}(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},t) := \frac{V(0) - V(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},t)}{V(0)} \times 100\%.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-944: We also define the efficacy similarly as \begin{equation}\operatorname{efficacy}(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},t) := \frac{V(t) - V(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},t)}{V(t)} \times 100\%.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-945: In particular, the efficacy represents the extent of tumour density shrinkage throughout its growth course in comparison to no treatment, whereas the TCR reveals how much the tumour density has reduced since the commencement of treatment.
elements.sent-2411-946: We see that the TCR and efficacy are linearly related, so that an increase in treatment efficacy results in increased TCR, and vice-versa, via the formula \begin{equation}\operatorname{efficacy}(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},t) = \left(1 - \frac{V(0)}{V(t)}\right) \times 100\% + \frac{V(0)}{V(t)} \times \operatorname{TCR}\left(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},t\right). \label{tvreff}\end{equation} We can also consider the efficiency of the treatment regimen, with a dosing interval of $\eta_\mathrm{pembro}$ and dosage $\xi_\mathrm{pembro}$ given by \begin{equation}\operatorname{efficiency}(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},t) := \frac{\operatorname{TCR}(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},t)}{\xi_\mathrm{pembro}\left(1+\lfloor \min(t,84)/\eta_\mathrm{pembro}\rfloor\right)},\end{equation} where $\xi_\mathrm{pembro}\left(1+\lfloor \min(t,84)/\eta_\mathrm{pembro}\rfloor\right)$ is the total dose of pembrolizumab administered by time $t$.
elements.sent-2411-947: This corresponds to the ratio between the TCR percentage and the total dose of pembrolizumab administered.
elements.sent-2411-948: There are two distinct treatment regimens for pembrolizumab therapy that are approved by the FDA for metastatic MSI-H/dMMR CRC in adults \cite{fda_pembrolizumab :.}
elements.sent-2411-951: We consider linearly spaced dosages in the domain $\xi_\mathrm{pembro \in [1\mathrm{~mg/kg}$, $10\mathrm{~mg/kg ]$, corresponding to $\xi_\mathrm{pembro \in [80\mathrm{~mg , 800\mathrm{~mg ]$, assuming a patient mass of $80 \mathrm{~kg}$, with an increment of $1 \mathrm{~mg}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-952: We also assume, as with Treatments 1 and 2, that pembrolizumab is administered as an intravenous infusion over a duration of 30 minutes, so that $\Delta_\mathrm{pembro = 2.08 \times 10^{-2 \mathrm{~day}$.}}
elements.figure-2411-032: [H] \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{pembroplots.png} \caption{\label{pembroplots}TCR (top left), efficacy (top right), efficiency (bottom left), and toxicity (bottom right) at $24$ weeks for $\eta_\mathrm{pembro}$ in the domain $\set{1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 12, 14, 21, 28, 42, 84, \infty}$ days. We sweep across linearly spaced $\xi_\mathrm{pembro}$ dosages between $80\mathrm{~mg}$ and $800\mathrm{~mg}$ with an increment of $1 \mathrm{~mg}$. The FDA-approved regimens (Treatment 1 and Treatment 2) for metastatic MSI-H/dMMR CRC are shown in black, and the optimal treatment regimen for locally advanced MSI-H/dMMR CRC is shown in blue.}
elements.sent-2411-953: We can determine the optimal pembrolizumab therapy by considering the regimen that achieves an acceptable TCR at $24$ weeks whilst maximising treatment efficiency as much as possible and ensuring a toxicity of less than 1.
elements.sent-2411-954: The TCR of Treatment 1 at $24$ weeks was calculated to be $89.68\%$, and the TCR of Treatment 2 at $24$ weeks was calculated to be $92.98\%$.
elements.sent-2411-955: As such, to ensure that the TCR of the optimal treatment is comparable to current FDA-approved pembrolizumab regimens, we set the threshold TCR to be $89\%$.
elements.sent-2411-956: Denoting the space of $(\xi_\mathrm{pembro ,\eta_\mathrm{pembro )$ pairs that satisfy these criteria as $\mathcal{S ^\mathrm{prac}$, the optimal pembrolizumab dosing and spacing, denoted $\xi_\mathrm{pembro ^\mathrm{opt}$ and $\eta_\mathrm{pembro ^\mathrm{opt}$, satisfy \begin{equation}\left(\xi_\mathrm{pembro}^\mathrm{opt},\eta_\mathrm{pembro}^\mathrm{opt}\right) = \underset{\substack{\operatorname{TCR}(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},168) \geq 89\% (\xi_\mathrm{pembro},\eta_\mathrm{pembro}) \in \mathcal{S}^\mathrm{prac} \operatorname{toxicity}(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},168)\leq 1}}{\operatorname{argmax}} \ \operatorname{efficiency}\left(\xi_\mathrm{pembro}; \eta_\mathrm{pembro}, 168\right). \label{pembroopteqn}\end{equation} Solving \eqref{pembroopteqn leads to the optimal therapy occuring when $\xi_\mathrm{pembro ^\mathrm{opt = 640 \mathrm{~mg}$ and $\eta_\mathrm{pembro ^\mathrm{opt = \infty \mathrm{~days}$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-957: This corresponds to a single dose of $640 \mathrm{~mg}$, corresponding to $8 \mathrm{~mg/kg}$, and results in a TCR of $89.06\%$, an efficacy of $94.09\%$, an efficiency of $1.39 \times 10^{-1 \mathrm{\%/mg}$, and a toxicity of $3.11 \times 10^{-1}$ at $24$ weeks.}

elements.sent-2411-966: It is difficult to compare our results to that of pre-existing clinical trials for locally advanced MSI-H/dMMR CRC due to the lack of time-series data, widely varying treatment regimens tested, and the broad range of outcomes found.

elements.sent-2411-973: To explain this, we first note that with treatment, the concentration of cancer cells decreases significantly over time, leading to reduced levels of DAMPs, as well as lower concentrations of TGF-$\upbeta$ and IL-10.
elements.sent-2411-974: This leads to reduced maturation of DCs, which in turn results in decreased activation of naive T cells in the TDLN, subsequently leading to lower concentrations of effector T cells in both the TDLN and TS.
elements.sent-2411-975: This also leads to reduced activation of NK cells and polarisation of macrophages.
elements.sent-2411-976: As a result, the concentration of activated immune cells decreases over time, resulting in decreased rates of secretion of many cytokines, including TNF, since we assumed that the influx of naive immune cells remains constant over time.
elements.sent-2411-977: This also causes the concentration of immunosuppressive cell types, such as M2 macrophages and CAFs, to decrease with pembrolizumab therapy relative to the concentrations without treatment.
elements.sent-2411-978: At 12 weeks, the concentration of IL-2 without treatment is $2.43 \times 10^{-12 \mathrm{~g/cm^3}$, compared to $2.49 \times 10^{-12 \mathrm{~g/cm^3}$, $2.48 \times 10^{-12 \mathrm{~g/cm^3}$, and $2.47 \times 10^{-12 \mathrm{~g/cm^3}$ with Treatments 1 and 2, and the optimal treatment respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-979: We note, however, that at 24 weeks, the concentration of IL-2 decreases with pembrolizumab therapy as a result of the reduction in effector CD8+ T cells in the TS.
elements.sent-2411-980: With the optimal treatment, and with Treatments 1 and 2, at 24 weeks, the concentration of PD-1 in the TS is reduced by 16.63\%, 17.77\%, and 16.31\%, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-981: This makes sense since PD-1 inhibits T cell activation in the TDLN, inhibits the cytotoxicity of NK cells, and interacts with PD-L1 to form the PD-1/PD-L1 complex, which inhibits T cell proliferation.
elements.sent-2411-982: For most of the treatment period, the optimal regimen yields the highest TCR and efficacy; however, it is eventually surpassed by Treatments 1 and 2.
elements.sent-2411-983: This occurs since the optimal regimen leads to the greatest pembrolizumab concentrations for the first several weeks, resulting in enhanced exhausted CD8+ T cell reinvigoration and PD-1 depletion.
elements.sent-2411-984: In particular, since Treatments 1 and 2 are not single-dose, pembrolizumab concentrations are replenished with each dose so that the resultant effects occur at lower potency but for a longer period of time.
elements.sent-2411-985: This also explains why Treatment 1, the treatment with the lowest pembrolizumab dosages, is the most efficient during the first three weeks.
elements.sent-2411-986: However, following the administration of a second dose, the other treatments demonstrate greater efficiency, with the optimal treatment becoming much more efficient as the treatment progresses.
elements.sent-2411-987: Finally, as expected, the toxicity of all treatments is generally a non-increasing function of time, but if the pembrolizumab concentration is sufficiently high enough, small spikes in toxicity may occur following dose administration.
elements.sent-2411-988: We see that TCR increases as the dosing increases and spacing decreases.
elements.sent-2411-989: In particular, the TCRs of Treatment 1 and Treatment 2 are high, and the TCR of treatments does not change significantly near these regions.
elements.sent-2411-990: Whilst these treatments are amongst the most efficient practical ones, they pale in comparison to the global maximum efficiency of $2.18 \times 10^{-1 \mathrm{\%/mg}$, achieved by administering a single $262 \mathrm{~mg}$ dose of pembrolizumab.}
elements.sent-2411-991: In particular, treatment is inefficient if its TCR is low, regardless of the dosing and spacing (corresponding to the top left inefficient region in \autoref{pembroplots ), or if an excessive amount of pembrolizumab is administered, regardless of the TCR (corresponding to the bottom right inefficient region in \autoref{pembroplots ).}}
elements.sent-2411-992: As expected, Treatments 1 and 2 are non-toxic, with toxicities of $1.86 \times 10^{-1}$ and $2.53 \times 10^{-1}$, respectively, whilst the optimal regimen has a toxicity of $3.12 \times 10^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-993: The optimal regimen of administering pembrolizumab as a single dose of $640 \mathrm{~mg}$ is also more efficient, leads to comparable TCR, and is more cost-effective and convenient than current regimens, whilst again, maintaining practicality and safety.
elements.sent-2411-994: Moreover, lower single doses of pembrolizumab are effective, with a single dose of $400\mathrm{~mg}$ (equivalent to $5\mathrm{~mg/kg}$) achieving a TCR of $76.80\%$, and a single dose of $560\mathrm{~mg}$ (equivalent to $7\mathrm{~mg/kg}$) achieving a TCR of $86.43\%$.
elements.sent-2411-995: The associated toxicities are $1.94 \times 10^{-1}$ and $2.72 \times 10^{-1}$, respectively, which are lower than or comparable to those of Treatments 1 and 2.

elements.sent-2411-1000: In this work, we have provided a framework for mathematically modelling many immune cell types in the TME, using experimental data to govern parameter estimation.
elements.sent-2411-1001: We finally analysed and optimised neoadjuvant pembrolizumab therapy for MSI-H/dMMR CRC for efficacy and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-1002: We conclude that a single medium-to-high dose of pembrolizumab is more efficient and demonstrates comparable efficacy and TCR than current FDA-approved regimens for metastatic MSI-H/dMMR CRC whilst maintaining practicality and safety.

elements.sent-2411-1134: We finally optimised neoadjuvant treatment with pembrolizumab, a widely used PD-1 inhibitor, to balance efficacy, efficiency, and toxicity in locally advanced MSI-H/dMMR CRC patients.
elements.sent-2411-1135: We mechanistically analysed factors influencing treatment success and improved upon currently FDA-approved therapeutic regimens for metastatic MSI-H/dMMR CRC, demonstrating that a single medium-to-high dose of pembrolizumab may be sufficient for effective tumour eradication while being efficient, safe and practical.
elements.sent-2411-1136: Colorectal cancer (CRC) is the third most common cancer worldwide, accounting for approximately $10\%$ of all cancer cases \cite{Klimeck2023 , with more than 1.85 million cases and 850,000 deaths annually \cite{Biller2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-1137: The American Cancer Society estimates that in the United States, there will be 152,810 new cases of CRC diagnosed and 53,010 deaths due to CRC \cite{Siegel2024 , with individual risk factors including a family history of CRC, inflammatory bowel disease, and type 2 diabetes \cite{Gausman2020 .}}
elements.sent-2411-1138: However, the response rate of advanced CRC patients with 5-fluorouracil monotherapy remains at only 10--15\%, with the addition of other anti-cancer drugs increasing response rates to only 40--50\% \cite{Gu2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-1139: This leads to a highly increased mutational rate, with MSI-H/dMMR CRC tumours having 10--100 times more somatic mutations compared to microsatellite stable (MSS) CRC tumours \cite{MuletMargalef2023 , resulting in increased tumour mutation burden (TMB) and neoantigen load, and immunogenic tumour microenvironment (TME) with dense immune cell infiltration \cite{Llosa2015, Giannakis2016 .}}
elements.sent-2411-1140: This immunogenicity results in patients with MSI-H/dMMR CRC having a good prognosis for immunotherapy treatment, in particular to immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) \cite{Ciardiello2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-1141: When PD-1 interacts with its ligands (PD-L1 and PD-L2), effector T cell activity is inhibited, resulting in the downregulation of pro-inflammatory cytokine secretion and the upregulation of immunosuppressive regulatory T cells (Tregs) \cite{Lin2024, han2020pd .}
elements.sent-2411-1142: One such phase II study is the NEOPRISM-CRC, NCT05197322, 31 patients with a high TMR and high-risk stage II or stage III MSI-H/dMMR CRC were given three cycles of pembrolizumab, at a dose of 200 mg every 3 weeks via IV infusion, and underwent surgery 4--6 weeks after the last dose was administered \cite{Shiu2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-1143: Another phase II trial, NCT04082572, aimed to evaluate the efficacy of neoadjuvant pembrolizumab on localised MSI-H/dMMR solid tumours \cite{Ludford2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-1144: Surgery was performed after the last dose, with a pCR achieved in 53.8\% of patients, including 47.1\% of those who received one cycle and 68.0\% of those who received two cycles.
elements.sent-2411-1145: However, in the RESET-C study, 84 patients with resectable stage I-III dMMR colon cancer received a single neoadjuvant cycle of pembrolizumab at 4 mg/kg (up to a maximum of 400 mg), followed by surgical resection within three to five weeks and CT scans at one and three years for follow-up \cite{Qvortrup2025 .}
elements.sent-2411-1146: This regimen resulted in a major pathological response in 57\% of patients and a pCR rate of 44\%, including 33\% for stage III cancer, suggesting that a single cycle of neoadjuvant pembrolizumab may be sufficient to achieve pCR in some locally advanced colon cancer patients, particularly those with earlier-stage disease.
elements.sent-2411-1147: Kirshtein et al. developed an ordinary differential equation (ODE) model of CRC progression incorporating immunological components such as T helper cells, Tregs, dendritic cells (DCs), and macrophages, and they considered the effects of carcinogenic cytokines and immunosuppressive agents \cite{Kirshtein2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-1148: Quantitative systems pharmacology (QSP) models have also been used to model clinical responses to ICI therapy, with a QSP model by Milberg et al. simulating the dynamics of immune cell interactions and PD-1 binding to its ligands, PD-L1 and PD-L2, across multiple physiological compartments, including the spleen, tumour microenvironment, and lymph nodes \cite{Milberg2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-1149: Kumbhari et al. attempted to address this in \cite{Kumbhari2020 and \cite{Kumbhari2020n2 in the context of optimising cancer vaccine therapy; however, activation is treated as occurring instantaneously, which has been shown to be false experimentally \cite{Plambeck2022 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-1150: To date, DC maturation is considered only by HMGB1 in some models, with other important DAMPs, such as calreticulin, not being taken into account \cite{DelPrete2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-1151: Furthermore, no deterministic model to date has adequately addressed CD8+ T cell exhaustion due to prolonged antigen exposure \cite{Blank2019 nor their potential reinvigoration through immune checkpoint blockade.}
elements.sent-2411-1152: Likewise, no immunobiological model has accurately represented the interactions between PD-1 and pembrolizumab.
elements.sent-2411-1153: Whilst models such as \cite{Zhang2024model by Zhang et al. consider PD-1 on PD-1-expressing cells separately, immunobiological models universally treat the effect of PD-1 as a mass-action depletion rather than accounting for the biologically more accurate processes of formation, dissociation, and internalisation of the PD-1/pembrolizumab complex.}
elements.sent-2411-1154: Whilst QSP models take these into account and integrate numerous immune processes and interactions, their large scale and complexity often pose a major drawback by limiting the ability to derive clear mechanistic insights.
elements.sent-2411-1155: This is a simplification since locally advanced CRC typically involves multiple tumour-draining lymph nodes \cite{Wang2024 ; however, for simplicity, we focus on the sentinel node and refer to it as the TDLN for the purposes of the model.}

elements.sent-2411-1157: We assume that all species, $X_i$, degrade/die at a rate proportional to their concentration, with decay constant $d_{X_i}$.
elements.sent-2411-1158: Effector CD8+ T cells and NK cells induce apoptosis of cancer cells with this being inhibited by TGF-$\upbeta$ and the PD-1/PD-L1 complex.
elements.sent-2411-1159: Some mature DCs migrate to the T cell zone of the TDLN and activate naive CD8+ and CD4+ T cells (including Tregs), with CD8+ T cell and Th1 cell activation being inhibited by Tregs and the PD-1/PD-L1 complex.
elements.sent-2411-1160: Activated T cells undergo clonal expansion and proliferate rapidly in the TDLN, with CD8+ T cell and Th1 cell proliferation being inhibited by Tregs and the PD-1/PD-L1 complex.
elements.sent-2411-1161: T cells that have completed proliferation migrate to the TS and perform effector functions including the production of pro-inflammatory (IL-2, IFN-$\upgamma$, TNF) and immunosuppressive (TGF-$\upbeta$, IL-10) cytokines.
elements.sent-2411-1162: In addition, mature DCs, NK cells and macrophages secrete cytokines that can activate NK cells and polarise and repolarise macrophages into pro-inflammatory and immunosuppressive phenotypes.
elements.sent-2411-1163: Pembrolizumab infusion promotes the binding of free PD-1 receptors to pembrolizumab, forming the PD-1/pembrolizumab complex instead of the PD-1/PD-L1 complex.
elements.sent-2411-1164: This reduces the inhibition of pro-inflammatory CD8+ and Th1 cell activation and proliferation while also reducing the inhibition of cancer cell lysis.
elements.sent-2411-1165: Viable cancer cells are killed by effector CD8+ T cells \cite{Raskov2020 and activated NK cells \cite{Zhang2020 through direct contact, whilst TNF and IFN-$\upgamma$ indirectly eliminate cancer cells via activating cell death pathways \cite{Josephs2018, Wang2008, Jorgovanovic2020 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-1166: We note that TGF-$\upbeta$ and the PD-1/PD-L1 complex inhibits cancer cell lysis by CD8+ T cells \cite{Thomas2005, Juneja2017, Azuma2008 , and that TGF-$\upbeta$ and PD-1/PD-L1 has been shown to inhibit NK cell cytotoxicity \cite{Regis2020, Batlle2019, Hsu2018, Lin2024, Quatrini2020, Liu2017 .}}

elements.sent-2411-1172: Recalling that T cells that have become activated by mature DCs are no longer naive, and taking this all into account, leads to \begin{equation}\frac{dT_0^8}{dt}=\underbrace{\mathcal{A}_{T_0^8}}_{\text{source}} -\underbrace{R^8(t)}_{\substack{\text{CD8+ T cell} \text{activation}}} - \underbrace{d_{T_0^8}T_0^8}_{\text{death}}, \label{naivecd8eqn}\end{equation} where $R^8(t)$ is defined as \begin{equation}R^8(t) := \underbrace{\frac{\lambda_{T_0^8 T_A^8}e^{-d_{T_0^8}\tau_8^\mathrm{act}}D^\mathrm{LN}(t-\tau_8^\mathrm{act})T_0^8(t-\tau_8^\mathrm{act})}{\left(1+\int_{t-\tau_8^\mathrm{act}}^{t} T_A^{r}(s) \ ds/K_{T_0^8T_A^r}\right)\left(1+\int_{t-\tau_8^\mathrm{act}}^{t} Q^{8\mathrm{LN}}(s) \ ds/K_{T_0^8Q^{8\mathrm{LN}}}\right)}}_{\text{CD8+ T cell activation inhibited by $T_A^r$ and $Q^{8\mathrm{LN}}$}}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-328: \begin{equation}R^8(t) := \underbrace{\frac{\lambda_{T_0^8 T_A^8}e^{-d_{T_0^8}\tau_8^act}D^LN(t-\tau_8^act)T_0^8(t-\tau_8^act)}{\left(1+\int_{t-\tau_8^act}^{t} T_A^{r}(s) \ ds/K_{T_0^8T_A^r}\right)\left(1+\int_{t-\tau_8^act}^{t} Q^{8LN}(s) \ ds/K_{T_0^8Q^{8LN}}\right)}}_{\text{CD8+ T cell activation inhibited by $T_A^r$ and $Q^{8LN}$}}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1173: In particular, since effector Tregs and the PD-1/PD-L1 complex inhibit T cell activation during the whole activation process, it is not sufficient to consider point estimates of effector Treg and PD-1/PD-L1 concentration.
elements.sent-2411-1174: Instead, we resort to considering the integrals of the concentrations of the relevant species throughout the entire $\tau_8^\mathrm{act}$ time that the CD8+ T cell takes to complete activation.
elements.sent-2411-1175: This is because these integrals are proportional (with a proportionality constant of $1/{\tau_8^\mathrm{act }$) to the average concentration of these species throughout activation, allowing us to properly incorporate its inhibition by effector Tregs and the PD-1/PD-L1 complex.}
elements.sent-2411-1176: Furthermore, we assume a constant cell cycle time $\Delta_8$, except for the first cell division, which has a cycle time $\Delta_8^0$.
elements.sent-2411-1177: Thus, the duration of the activated CD8+ T cell division program to $n^8_\mathrm{max}$ divisions is given by \begin{equation}\tau_{T_A^8} := \Delta_8^0 + (n^8_\mathrm{max}-1)\Delta_8.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1178: In particular, we must take into account that some T cells will die before the division program is complete, so we must introduce a shrinkage factor of $e^{-d_{T_0^8}\tau_{T_A^8 }$.}

elements.subsubsection-2411-106: Equations for free PD-1 receptors on cells in the TS ($P_D^{T_8}
elements.sent-2411-1206: We assume that the rate of PD-1 synthesis is proportional to the concentration of the cell expressing it.
elements.sent-2411-1207: However, free PD-1 receptors on these PD-1-expressing cells can bind to either pembrolizumab or PD-L1, forming the PD-1/pembrolizumab and PD-1/PD-L1 complexes, respectively, resulting in the depletion of free PD-1 molecules \cite{han2020pd, Lin2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-1208: For simplicity, we assume that the formation and dissociation rates of the PD-1/PD-L1 and PD-1/pembrolizumab complexes are invariant of the type of cell expressing PD-1.
elements.sent-2411-1209: Considering free PD-1 receptors on effector CD8+ T cells in the TS at first, and taking into account the degradation of PD-1 receptors, this motivates the equation for $P_D^{T_8}$ to be \begin{equation}\frac{dP_D^{T_8}}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{P_D^{T_8}}T_8}_{synthesis} + \underbrace{\lambda_{Q_A}Q_A^{T_8}}_{\substack{dissociation \text{of $Q_A^{T_8}$}}} + \underbrace{\lambda_{Q}Q^{T_8}}_{\substack{dissociation \text{of $Q^{T_8}$}}} - \underbrace{\lambda_{P_DA_1}P_D^{T_8}A_1}_{binding to $A_1$} - \underbrace{\lambda_{P_DP_L}P_D^{T_8}P_L}_{binding to $P_L$} - \underbrace{d_{P_D}P_D^{T_8}}_{degradation}. \label{PD8eqntemp} \intertext{Similarly, we have that} \frac{dP_D^{T_1}}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{P_D^{T_1}}T_1}_{synthesis} + \underbrace{\lambda_{Q_A}Q_A^{T_1}}_{\substack{dissociation \text{of $Q_A^{T_1}$}}} + \underbrace{\lambda_{Q}Q^{T_1}}_{\substack{dissociation \text{of $Q^{T_1}$}}} - \underbrace{\lambda_{P_DA_1}P_D^{T_1}A_1}_{binding to $A_1$} - \underbrace{\lambda_{P_DP_L}P_D^{T_1}P_L}_{binding to $P_L$} - \underbrace{d_{P_D}P_D^{T_1}}_{degradation}, \label{PD1eqntemp} \frac{dP_D^{K}}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{P_D^{K}}K}_{synthesis} + \underbrace{\lambda_{Q_A}Q_A^{K}}_{\substack{dissociation \text{of $Q_A^{K}$}}} + \underbrace{\lambda_{Q}Q^{K}}_{\substack{dissociation \text{of $Q^{K}$}}} - \underbrace{\lambda_{P_DA_1}P_D^{K}A_1}_{binding to $A_1$} - \underbrace{\lambda_{P_DP_L}P_D^{K}P_L}_{binding to $P_L$} - \underbrace{d_{P_D}P_D^{K}}_{degradation}. \label{PDKeqntemp}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-343: \begin{equation}\frac{dP_D^{T_8}}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{P_D^{T_8}}T_8}_{synthesis} + \underbrace{\lambda_{Q_A}Q_A^{T_8}}_{\substack{dissociation \text{of $Q_A^{T_8}$}}} + \underbrace{\lambda_{Q}Q^{T_8}}_{\substack{dissociation \text{of $Q^{T_8}$}}} - \underbrace{\lambda_{P_DA_1}P_D^{T_8}A_1}_{binding to $A_1$} - \underbrace{\lambda_{P_DP_L}P_D^{T_8}P_L}_{binding to $P_L$} - \underbrace{d_{P_D}P_D^{T_8}}_{degradation}. \label{PD8eqntemp} \intertext{Similarly, we have that} \frac{dP_D^{T_1}}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{P_D^{T_1}}T_1}_{synthesis} + \underbrace{\lambda_{Q_A}Q_A^{T_1}}_{\substack{dissociation \text{of $Q_A^{T_1}$}}} + \underbrace{\lambda_{Q}Q^{T_1}}_{\substack{dissociation \text{of $Q^{T_1}$}}} - \underbrace{\lambda_{P_DA_1}P_D^{T_1}A_1}_{binding to $A_1$} - \underbrace{\lambda_{P_DP_L}P_D^{T_1}P_L}_{binding to $P_L$} - \underbrace{d_{P_D}P_D^{T_1}}_{degradation}, \label{PD1eqntemp} \frac{dP_D^{K}}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{P_D^{K}}K}_{synthesis} + \underbrace{\lambda_{Q_A}Q_A^{K}}_{\substack{dissociation \text{of $Q_A^{K}$}}} + \underbrace{\lambda_{Q}Q^{K}}_{\substack{dissociation \text{of $Q^{K}$}}} - \underbrace{\lambda_{P_DA_1}P_D^{K}A_1}_{binding to $A_1$} - \underbrace{\lambda_{P_DP_L}P_D^{K}P_L}_{binding to $P_L$} - \underbrace{d_{P_D}P_D^{K}}_{degradation}. \label{PDKeqntemp}\end{equation}
elements.subsubsection-2411-107: Equations for the PD-1/pembrolizumab complex on cells in the TS ($Q_A^{T_8}
elements.sent-2411-1210: We must also account for loss due to the endocytosis and internalisation of the PD-1/pembrolizumab complex from the surface of cells \cite{Cowles2022, BenSaad2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-1211: We assume that the rates of PD-1/pembrolizumab complex internalisation and dissociation are invariant of the type of cell expressing PD-1 so that \begin{equation}\frac{dQ_A^{T_8}}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{P_DA_1}P_D^{T_8}A_1}_{\text{formation of $Q_A^{T_8}$}} - \underbrace{\lambda_{Q_A}Q_A^{T_8}}_{\text{dissociation of $Q_A^{T_8}$}} - \underbrace{d_{Q_A}Q_A^{T_8}}_{internalisation}, \label{QA8eqn} \frac{dQ_A^{T_1}}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{P_DA_1}P_D^{T_1}A_1}_{\text{formation of $Q_A^{T_1}$}} - \underbrace{\lambda_{Q_A}Q_A^{T_1}}_{\text{dissociation of $Q_A^{T_1}$}} - \underbrace{d_{Q_A}Q_A^{T_1}}_{internalisation}, \label{QA1eqn} \frac{dQ_A^{K}}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{P_DA_1}P_D^{K}A_1}_{\text{formation of $Q_A^{K}$}} - \underbrace{\lambda_{Q_A}Q_A^{K}}_{\text{dissociation of $Q_A^{K}$}} - \underbrace{d_{Q_A}Q_A^{K}}_{internalisation}. \label{QAKeqn}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-344: \begin{equation}\frac{dQ_A^{T_8}}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{P_DA_1}P_D^{T_8}A_1}_{\text{formation of $Q_A^{T_8}$}} - \underbrace{\lambda_{Q_A}Q_A^{T_8}}_{\text{dissociation of $Q_A^{T_8}$}} - \underbrace{d_{Q_A}Q_A^{T_8}}_{internalisation}, \label{QA8eqn} \frac{dQ_A^{T_1}}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{P_DA_1}P_D^{T_1}A_1}_{\text{formation of $Q_A^{T_1}$}} - \underbrace{\lambda_{Q_A}Q_A^{T_1}}_{\text{dissociation of $Q_A^{T_1}$}} - \underbrace{d_{Q_A}Q_A^{T_1}}_{internalisation}, \label{QA1eqn} \frac{dQ_A^{K}}{dt} = \underbrace{\lambda_{P_DA_1}P_D^{K}A_1}_{\text{formation of $Q_A^{K}$}} - \underbrace{\lambda_{Q_A}Q_A^{K}}_{\text{dissociation of $Q_A^{K}$}} - \underbrace{d_{Q_A}Q_A^{K}}_{internalisation}. \label{QAKeqn}\end{equation}
elements.subsubsection-2411-108: Equation for Pembrolizumab in the TS ($A_1$)
elements.sent-2411-1212: We assume that pembrolizumab is administered intravenously at times $t_1$, $t_2$, \dots, $t_n$ with doses $\xi_{1}$, $\xi_{2}$, \dots, $\xi_{n}$ respectively, assuming that the duration of infusion is negligible in comparison to the time period of interest.
elements.sent-2411-1213: We also account for pembrolizumab depletion due to binding to free PD-1, replenishing due to PD-1/pembrolizumab complex dissociation, and elimination of pembrolizumab.
elements.sent-2411-1214: It is important to note that the administered dose is not equal to the corresponding change in concentration in the TS.
elements.sent-2411-1215: For simplicity, we assume linear pharmacokinetics so that, for some scaling factor $f_\mathrm{pembro}$, we have that \begin{equation}\frac{dA_{1}}{dt} =\underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \xi_{j}f_\mathrm{pembro}\delta\left(t-t_j \right)}_{\text{infusion}} + \underbrace{\lambda_{Q_A}\left(Q_A^{T_8}+ Q_A^{T_1} + Q_A^{K}\right)}_{\text{dissociation of $Q_A^{T_8}$, $Q_A^{T_1}$, and $Q_A^{K}$}} - \underbrace{\lambda_{P_DA_1}\left(P_D^{T_8} + P_D^{T_1} + P_D^{K}\right)A_1}_{\text{formation of $Q_A^{T_8}$, $Q_A^{T_1}$, and $Q_A^{K}$}} - \underbrace{d_{A_1}A_{1}}_{\text{elimination}}. \label{A1eqn}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-345: \begin{equation}\frac{dA_{1}}{dt} =\underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \xi_{j}f_pembro\delta\left(t-t_j \right)}_{infusion} + \underbrace{\lambda_{Q_A}\left(Q_A^{T_8}+ Q_A^{T_1} + Q_A^{K}\right)}_{\text{dissociation of $Q_A^{T_8}$, $Q_A^{T_1}$, and $Q_A^{K}$}} - \underbrace{\lambda_{P_DA_1}\left(P_D^{T_8} + P_D^{T_1} + P_D^{K}\right)A_1}_{\text{formation of $Q_A^{T_8}$, $Q_A^{T_1}$, and $Q_A^{K}$}} - \underbrace{d_{A_1}A_{1}}_{elimination}. \label{A1eqn}\end{equation}
elements.subsubsection-2411-109: Equation for free PD-L1 in the TS ($P_L$)

elements.sent-2411-1229: One thing to note is that activated CD8+ T cells and Th1 cells also express PD-1 receptors and PD-L1 ligands, and we assume that effector and activated cells express these in equal amounts.

elements.sent-2411-1231: Since the PD-1/PD-L1 complex concentration is linearly proportional to the product of PD-L1 concentration and free PD-1 receptor concentration, and PD-1/PD-L1-mediated inhibition of T cell proliferation in the TDLN appears only as a ratio, it is sufficient to consider only PD-1, PD-L1, and PD-1/PD-L1 concentrations on effector cells, as this will be appropriately scaled by the corresponding inhibition constants.
elements.sent-2411-1232: Furthermore, this also justifies using the PD-1/PD-L1 complex concentration on effector T cells as a proxy for its concentration on activated T cells that have not yet undergone division, given that their ratio to effector cells remains roughly constant and that PD-1/PD-L1-mediated inhibition of T cell activation in the TDLN appears only as a ratio.

elements.sent-2411-1245: To estimate the TNM stages of the patients with lymph node metastases, we considered the samples of lymph node metastases for these patients and applied the ImmuCellAI algorithm to estimate their immune cell abundances, ignoring Tcm and Tem cell subtypes.

elements.sent-2411-1248: Like before, we used lymph node metastases from stage IIIC/IVA patients to infer TDLN steady states.

elements.sent-2411-1263: We define the TCR from this regimen to be \begin{equation}\operatorname{TCR}(\xi_pembro;\eta_pembro,t) := \frac{V(0) - V(\xi_pembro;\eta_pembro,t)}{V(0)} \times 100\%. \intertext{We also define the efficacy similarly as} \operatorname{efficacy}(\xi_pembro;\eta_pembro,t) := \frac{V(t) - V(\xi_pembro;\eta_pembro,t)}{V(t)} \times 100\%.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-357: \begin{equation}\operatorname{TCR}(\xi_pembro;\eta_pembro,t) := \frac{V(0) - V(\xi_pembro;\eta_pembro,t)}{V(0)} \times 100\%. \intertext{We also define the efficacy similarly as} \operatorname{efficacy}(\xi_pembro;\eta_pembro,t) := \frac{V(t) - V(\xi_pembro;\eta_pembro,t)}{V(t)} \times 100\%.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1264: We see that the TCR and efficacy are linearly related so that an increase in treatment efficacy results in increased TCR, and vice-versa, via the formula \begin{equation}\operatorname{efficacy}(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},t) = \left(1 - \frac{V(0)}{V(t)}\right) \times 100\% + \frac{V(0)}{V(t)} \times \operatorname{TCR}\left(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},t\right). \label{tcreff}\end{equation} We can also consider the efficiency of the treatment regimen, with a dosing interval of $\eta_\mathrm{pembro}$ weeks and dosage $\xi_\mathrm{pembro}$ mg/kg given by \begin{equation}\operatorname{efficiency}(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},t) := \frac{\operatorname{TCR}(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},t)}{\xi_\mathrm{pembro}m\left(\lfloor \min\left(t,84\right)/7\eta_\mathrm{pembro}\rfloor + \theta(84-t)\right)},\end{equation} where $\theta(t)$ is the Heaviside function which equals $1$ if $t\geq 0$, and $0$ otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-1265: In particular, $\xi_\mathrm{pembro m\left(\lfloor \min\left(t,84\right)/7\eta_\mathrm{pembro}\rfloor + \theta(84-t)\right)$ is the total dose of pembrolizumab administered by time $t$, recalling that no treatment is given for $t \geq 84$ days.}
elements.sent-2411-1266: To rigorise this notion, we define the toxicity of the treatment regimen, with a dosing interval of $\eta_\mathrm{pembro}$ weeks and dosage $\xi_\mathrm{pembro}$ mg/kg, as \begin{equation}\operatorname{toxicity}(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},t) := \max\left( \frac{\underset{s \in [0,t]}{\max} \, A_1(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},s)}{\underset{s \in [0,t]}{\max} \, A_1(10;2,s)}, \frac{\underset{s \in [0,t]}{\max} \, A_1^\mathrm{LN}(\xi_\mathrm{pembro};\eta_\mathrm{pembro},s)}{\underset{s \in [0,t]}{\max} \, A_1^\mathrm{LN}(10;2,s)} \right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1267: In particular, $A_1(\xi_\mathrm{pembro ;\eta_\mathrm{pembro ,s)$ and $A_1^\mathrm{LN (\xi_\mathrm{pembro ;\eta_\mathrm{pembro ,s)$ denote the concentrations of $A_1$ and $A_1^\mathrm{LN}$ at time $s$, with pembrolizumab doses of $\xi_\mathrm{pembro}$ at a dosing interval of $\eta_\mathrm{pembro}$, respectively.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-1274: This corresponds to $\xi_j \in [0.1m, 10m] \mathrm{~mg = [8, 800] \mathrm{~mg}$ with an increment of $1 \mathrm{~mg}$.}
elements.sent-2411-1275: The TCRs of Treatment 1 and Treatment 2 at $18$ weeks were calculated to be $86.47\%$ and $86.71\%$, respectively.
